The 33% radius of the non-dominant arm is the ISCD-designated diagnostic forearm site, used for primary hyperparathyroidism monitoring and as an alternative when central sites are invalid.

front: A phantom QC failure due to severe room temperature fluctuations highlights the importance of:
back:
Environmental controls on equipment stability

DXA scanner electronics and detectors are sensitive to temperature and humidity. Sudden room temperature changes can shift calibration, causing QC failures. Manufacturers specify operating temperature ranges (typically 65-77 degrees F with controlled humidity). Climate-controlled scan rooms are part of equipment maintenance, distinct from patient care or informatics domains.
source: Manufacturer environmental specifications; ISCD QC standards

front: To ensure patient DXA data is not lost in the event of hardware failure, the technologist must adhere to facility protocols regarding:
back:
File and database management including local and network backups

ARRT 2022 BD Content Specifications explicitly include file and database management under Image Production. This encompasses local database backups, network/PACS archiving, retrieval of prior studies for follow-up comparison, and protection against data loss from hardware failure. Patient scan files must be retained per facility and regulatory requirements (typically minimum 5-7 years).
source: ARRT 2022 BD Content Specifications, Image Production

front: Which physiologic factor most dramatically influences density loss in perimenopausal women?
back:
Decreasing estrogen levels

Perimenopausal and post-menopausal estrogen withdrawal is the primary driver of female bone loss. Estrogen normally suppresses RANKL and increases OPG, restraining osteoclast activity. Loss of estrogen unleashes osteoclasts, accelerating bone resorption and producing peak bone loss of 1-3% per year in the first 5-10 years after menopause. This is the basis for HRT's effect on BMD and the rationale for treating early menopause patients.

front: A short femoral neck or inadequate space between the ischium and femur often leads to:
back:
Poor bone edge detection and inappropriate neck box placement

Variant pelvic anatomy (short femoral neck, narrow ischio-femoral space, or unusual pelvic geometry) compromises the algorithm's ability to distinguish bone edges, particularly between the femoral neck and ischium. This produces incorrect neck box placement and falsely elevated BMD from overlapping bone. The contralateral hip or alternative site may be required.
source: ISCD 2023 hip acquisition; manufacturer positioning guides

front: In a VFA scan, sclerotic or lytic changes are noted. The technologist must recognize:
back:
VFA is designed to detect vertebral fractures only; these findings require formal radiographic follow-up

VFA visualizes vertebral morphology for fracture grading (Genant semi-quantitative) but lacks the resolution to characterize sclerotic, lytic, or other lesions. Such findings warrant formal radiographic (or MRI) follow-up to rule out malignancy, infection, Paget's disease, or other pathology. The technologist's role is to flag the findings; characterization is the radiologist's role.

front: A technologist notices continuous longitudinal drift in external phantom data. What does this suggest?
back:
Gradual degradation or instability of the scanner's internal components requiring service

Steady longitudinal drift in phantom BMD values reflects scanner mechanical or electronic instability (detector aging, X-ray tube wear, calibration drift). This requires service investigation even if individual daily QC values remain within tolerance, because cumulative drift compromises serial monitoring accuracy. Phantoms are stable test objects, so any drift is scanner-related.
source: ISCD QC standards; manufacturer service protocols

front: The parameter 'Coefficient of Variation' (CV) is mathematically defined as:
back:
Standard deviation divided by the mean, expressed as a percentage

CV = (SD / Mean) x 100%. This normalizes precision error relative to the average BMD value, allowing comparison across patients with different baseline densities. A CV of 1% means the SD of repeated measurements is 1% of the mean. RMS-CV% combines individual CVs in a precision study to give the facility-wide precision error. LSC = 2.77 x RMS-SD (not multiplied by 2.77 from CV).

front: According to ISCD pediatric positions, bone size adjustment is sometimes recommended because:
back:
DXA overestimates BMD in tall children and underestimates it in short children due to its 2D projection

DXA measures areal BMD (BMC / projected area) which is fundamentally 2D. Smaller bones (short children) project less area, so areal BMD systematically underestimates true volumetric density. Conversely, larger bones in tall children may show artificially higher areal BMD. ISCD 2019 Pediatric Positions recommend adjustments such as BMAD (bone mineral apparent density) or height-for-age Z-score corrections in short or tall children.

topic: In vivo precision - monitoring time interval
front: When evaluating in vivo precision, the ISCD states that testing allows the facility to:
back:
Provide a monitoring time interval (MTI) to reliably measure true bone change over time

In vivo precision studies determine the facility's LSC, which then allows calculation of the Monitoring Time Interval (MTI): how long must elapse before expected biological change exceeds the LSC. For a 1% LSC and 1% expected annual change, MTI is ~1 year. Lower precision error gives shorter MTI, allowing earlier detection of true change. Phantom QC remains required.

front: If L2 is excluded due to a fracture, the reported total spine BMD is calculated from:
back:
L1, L3, and L4 vertebrae averaged

When a vertebra is excluded, the software recalculates the average BMD using only the remaining valid vertebrae. With L2 excluded, L1, L3, and L4 are averaged. ISCD requires at least two evaluable contiguous vertebrae; the L1, L3, L4 set contains the contiguous pair L3-L4 plus the non-contiguous L1, making the analysis valid.
source: ISCD 2023 Adult Official Positions, vertebral exclusion

front: To accurately apply the LSC threshold to a patient's scan, the technologist must ensure:
back:
The follow-up scan precisely matches the baseline parameters, positioning, and ROI placement

The LSC is meaningful only when baseline and follow-up scans are acquired with identical scanner, scan mode, patient positioning, and ROI placement. Any geometric or technical variation inflates measured difference above true biological change, invalidating the LSC threshold. The 'copy/compare' function on most scanners automates baseline parameter matching.

front: A VFA scan is specifically indicated for a patient taking glucocorticoids equivalent to:
back:
At least 5 mg prednisone per day for greater than 3 months

Per ISCD 2023, glucocorticoid therapy of at least 5 mg prednisone equivalent per day for at least 3 months is a high-risk indication for VFA, since glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis (GIO) causes rapid vertebral fracture even at normal BMD. Other VFA indications include height loss greater than 4 cm, age 70+ with T-score less than -1.0, and self-reported but undocumented prior vertebral fracture.
source: ISCD 2023 VFA indications; ACR GIO guidelines

front: During a spine DXA, a patient has severe scoliosis measuring 40 degrees. This presents a problem related to:
back:
ROI placement and analysis due to lateral curvature obscuring horizontal intervertebral spaces

Severe scoliosis (Cobb angle greater than 15-20 degrees) tilts vertebrae so that horizontal intervertebral lines cannot be cleanly placed in disc spaces. ROI boxes cannot align consistently across vertebrae, distorting area calculations and corrupting areal BMD. The hip or forearm is often more reliable in severe scoliosis. Mild scoliosis may still allow valid spine analysis with careful ROI placement.
source: ISCD 2023 spine analysis; manufacturer scoliosis guidance

front: Which bone type provides internal structural support and is highly metabolically active, making it the primary site of early osteoporotic loss?
back:
Cancellous (trabecular) bone

Cancellous (trabecular) bone has a much greater surface area than cortical bone, making it highly metabolically active and rapidly responsive to bone resorption. This is why early postmenopausal estrogen withdrawal preferentially depletes trabecular bone, and why the vertebral bodies (66% trabecular) show BMD loss earliest. Cortical bone is denser and changes more slowly.

front: The radiation exposure parameter 'dose equivalent' is typically measured in:
back:
Sieverts (Sv) or milliSieverts (mSv)

Dose equivalent (H = D x WR, where D is absorbed dose and WR is radiation weighting factor) is measured in Sieverts (Sv). For X-rays and gamma rays, WR = 1, so dose equivalent in Sv equals absorbed dose in Gy. Effective dose (also in Sv) further weights by tissue radiosensitivity. Becquerels and Curies measure radioactivity (decay events), not dose.

front: During a hip scan, a positioning aid (hip positioner) is used primarily to:
back:
Lock the feet in 15-25 degrees of internal rotation to elongate the femoral neck

The hip positioner is a foam or plastic device that holds the foot in 15-25 degrees of internal rotation, securing the femur in the standard position with the femoral neck parallel to the scan table. This produces the longest neck projection and hides the lesser trochanter posteriorly. Proper positioner use is essential for reproducible acquisitions across baseline and follow-up scans.
source: ISCD 2023 hip acquisition; manufacturer positioning guides

front: A routine lumbar spine scan reveals significant residual barium in the colon overlapping L2 and L3. What is the appropriate protocol?
back:
Analyze L1 and L4 only if L2/L3 cannot be assessed, or reschedule the patient

Barium is highly radiopaque and the algorithm cannot subtract it, so it falsely elevates BMC at affected vertebrae. Options are: exclude L2/L3 and use L1 plus L4 if at least 2 valid contiguous vertebrae remain; or reschedule the scan after barium clearance (typically 7-10 days). Drinking water does not clear residual barium quickly enough. Total body DXA is not diagnostic.
source: ISCD 2023 spine exclusion; standard artifact management

front: An anthropomorphic spine phantom is utilized for:
back:
Longitudinal quality control to mimic human tissue attenuation and track shift or drift

Anthropomorphic phantoms (Hologic's CaHA-in-epoxy spine phantom or GE's aluminum spine phantom) simulate bone and surrounding soft tissue attenuation. Daily phantom scans monitor scanner stability over time, detecting calibration shifts (sudden) or drifts (gradual). Phantom QC is the foundation of longitudinal monitoring confidence.
source: ISCD QC standards; Hologic and GE QC documentation

front: Which anatomic structure must NOT be included in the femoral neck ROI box?
back:
All of the above

The femoral neck ROI must capture only the femoral neck itself, centered across its narrowest portion perpendicular to the neck axis. Including the greater trochanter, ischium, or femoral shaft falsely alters both the projected area and BMC, producing incorrect BMD. The total hip ROI is broader and includes the trochanteric and intertrochanteric regions, but the femoral neck ROI is restricted.
source: ISCD 2023 hip acquisition; manufacturer ROI guides

front: Which of the following is considered an anabolic medication for osteoporosis?
back:
Teriparatide (Forteo) recombinant PTH 1-34

Teriparatide (Forteo, recombinant PTH 1-34) and abaloparatide (Tymlos, PTHrP analog) are anabolic agents that stimulate bone formation via intermittent PTH receptor activation. Romosozumab (Evenity, sclerostin inhibitor) is dual-action (anabolic + antiresorptive). Alendronate and denosumab are antiresorptive. The Forteo BBW for osteosarcoma and 2-year cap were removed November 2020.

front: Which action ensures the highest quality compliance when analyzing follow-up DXA scans?
back:
Systematically matching the follow-up image size, bone area, and baseline ROI placement

Valid follow-up monitoring requires the follow-up scan to match the baseline in scanner, scan mode, positioning, image size, projected bone area, and ROI placement. Most modern scanners offer 'copy/compare' to automatically load baseline parameters. Any geometric variation between scans inflates the measured difference above true biological change, invalidating LSC-based interpretation.
source: ISCD 2023 monitoring positions; manufacturer follow-up protocols

front: A patient's Z-score of -1.5 in a 12-year-old child means:
back:
Within the expected range for age

ISCD 2019 Pediatric Positions: Z-score above -2.0 is 'within the expected range for age,' regardless of how negative. A Z-score of -1.5 in a child is reassuring but does not preclude fragility, especially if the child has fracture history. Z-score of -2.0 or lower is 'below the expected range for age.' Pediatric osteoporosis requires both low Z-score AND clinically significant fracture history.

front: Does a Z-score of -1.5 preclude skeletal fragility in a child?
back:
No, a Z-score > -2.0 does not preclude skeletal fragility, especially with fracture history

Per ISCD 2019, a normal-range Z-score does not rule out skeletal fragility, especially in children with a significant fracture history (two or more long bone fractures by age 10, three by age 19, or any vertebral compression fracture without major trauma). Clinical assessment of fragility integrates BMD with fracture history; either alone is insufficient.

front: A VFA reveals a vertebra with a 45% reduction in anterior height. How is this graded using Genant's criteria?
back:
Grade 3 (Severe) fracture

Genant semi-quantitative grading: Grade 0 = normal (less than 20% height loss), Grade 1 = mild (20-25%), Grade 2 = moderate (25-40%), Grade 3 = severe (greater than 40%). A 45% reduction falls into Grade 3 (severe). Severe vertebral fractures have strong clinical implications and indicate high fracture risk regardless of BMD T-score.

front: A precision error of 0.015 g/cm2 is calculated. What is the absolute LSC (95% confidence)?
back:
0.042 g/cm2

LSC = 2.77 x precision error. With precision error of 0.015 g/cm2, LSC = 2.77 x 0.015 = 0.04155, rounded to 0.042 g/cm2. Any BMD change less than 0.042 g/cm2 between scans should be considered within measurement noise (not a true biological change) at 95% confidence. The 2.77 factor comes from 1.96 (95% z-score) x sqrt(2) for paired comparison.

front: When evaluating the proximal femur, the 'Ward's triangle' region is:
back:
Used only for research and not for diagnosis due to high precision error

Ward's triangle is a small, highly trabecular region within the femoral neck. While it has theoretical sensitivity to early bone loss, it has very poor reproducibility (its small size and shifting position produce high CV%). ISCD specifically excludes Ward's area from diagnostic use. The femoral neck and total hip are the standard hip diagnostic ROIs.

front: Which condition is most frequently associated with secondary osteoporosis?
back:
Primary hyperparathyroidism with elevated calcium

Primary hyperparathyroidism is the most common endocrine cause of secondary osteoporosis. Chronic PTH excess preferentially depletes cortical bone (33% radius is the worst-affected site). Workup includes serum calcium, intact PTH, 24-hour urine calcium, 25-OH vitamin D, creatinine, and DXA with 33% radius. Treatment is parathyroidectomy when indicated.

front: If L4 displays a dense, unremovable artifact due to a surgical fusion, the technologist must:
back:
Exclude L4 and report BMD based on L1-L3

Spinal fusion hardware (rods, screws, interbody cages) and bony fusion mass dramatically elevate BMC at affected vertebrae. The affected level must be excluded. With L4 excluded, L1, L2, and L3 (contiguous, three valid vertebrae) form the basis for diagnosis. ISCD requires at least two evaluable contiguous vertebrae for valid spine BMD.

front: What does an 'in vivo' precision study involve?
back:
Scanning a cohort of human subjects multiple times with repositioning

In vivo precision studies require scanning human subjects (typically 30 patients twice or 15 patients three times) with repositioning between scans. This captures both scanner variability AND operator positioning variability, both of which affect clinical follow-up scans. Phantom QC only measures scanner stability and underestimates true in vivo precision error.

front: Is formal, signed patient consent strictly required to conduct an in vivo precision assessment study according to ISCD?
back:
No, patients should be informed with right of refusal, but consent forms are not strictly required as it is quality assurance

ISCD classifies precision assessment as quality assurance rather than research, so formal written consent is not strictly required. Patients should be informed about the second scan and given the right to refuse. Most facilities provide brief verbal explanation. The radiation dose of two DXA scans is trivial (a few microSv), well below routine medical imaging thresholds.

front: A patient has a severe hip contracture preventing internal rotation. This is considered a:
back:
Positioning challenge that may lead to poor edge detection and invalid BMD

Hip contractures (often from hip arthritis, prior surgery, or neuromuscular disease) prevent the standard 15-25 degrees internal rotation, resulting in foreshortened femoral neck projection, prominent lesser trochanter, and falsely elevated BMD. The contralateral hip should be scanned if unaffected. If both hips are compromised, the lumbar spine plus 33% radius alternative protocol applies.

front: For diagnosing osteoporosis via central DXA, which site's lowest T-score is officially utilized?
back:
Lumbar spine, total hip, or femoral neck

Per ISCD 2023, osteoporosis diagnosis uses the LOWEST T-score from the standard diagnostic sites: lumbar spine (L1-L4), total hip, or femoral neck. When central sites are invalid, the 33% radius of the non-dominant forearm is the recognized alternative. Ward's area is not used. Total body BMD is for body composition, not diagnosis.

front: A scanner's 'drift' over time is defined as:
back:
A gradual, systematic change in calibration measured via longitudinal phantom scans

Drift is gradual, systematic deviation in scanner calibration over time (weeks to months), detected by trends in daily phantom QC data. A 'shift' is a sudden change (10+ consecutive points on one side of the mean). Both patterns require investigation, often resulting in service calls. Random patient motion is in vivo precision noise, not drift.
source: ISCD QC standards; Lewiecki 2016 J Clin Densitom

front: When a DXA scanner is physically relocated, what QC step must occur?
back:
Recalibration and re-establishment of baseline phantom data

Physical relocation disrupts the scanner's mechanical alignment and may shift detector calibration. After relocation, the scanner must be serviced, recalibrated, and a new phantom baseline established by scanning the phantom for 25 consecutive days (or per manufacturer protocol). Existing patient baselines may need cross-calibration if the scanner shifts significantly.
source: ISCD QC standards; manufacturer relocation protocols

front: The 2022 ARRT updates indicate technologists must understand 'Follow-up scanning'. This specifically targets:
back:
Matching acquisition parameters, positioning, and ROI placement to the baseline

Follow-up scanning under the 2022 ARRT specifications requires precise reproduction of baseline acquisition parameters: same scanner, same scan mode, same patient positioning, same ROI placement. Any variation between baseline and follow-up inflates measured difference above true biological change, invalidating LSC-based monitoring. The 'copy/compare' function automates baseline parameter matching.
source: ARRT 2022 BD Content Specifications; ISCD monitoring positions

topic: Spine vs hip with arthritis and hardware
front: A patient has severe osteoarthritis in the left hip and a right total hip replacement. Where should central DXA be performed?
back:
Lumbar spine (and 33% radius if needed)

Both hips are unreliable: the right has hardware (artifact invalidates BMD), and the left has severe arthritis (likely falsely elevates BMD via osteophytes and sclerosis). The lumbar spine is the appropriate central site, with the non-dominant 33% radius added if needed (and if the spine is also compromised by arthritis or surgery). ISCD endorses this multi-site assessment.

front: Which physiological factor is an intrinsic risk factor for falling?
back:
Impaired vision or vestibular balance

Intrinsic fall risk factors are patient-related: impaired vision, vestibular dysfunction, lower extremity weakness, polypharmacy (especially sedatives, antihypertensives), cognitive impairment, postural hypotension. Extrinsic factors are environmental: rugs, lighting, stairs, footwear, grab bars. Comprehensive fall prevention addresses both, but the question specifically asks about physiologic (intrinsic) factors.
source: BHOF 2022 Clinician's Guide; geriatric fall prevention literature

front: On a follow-up scan, the technologist notes the patient is positioned 2 inches higher on the table than baseline. This will likely:
back:
Cause precision error due to un-matched acquisition geometry

Positioning the patient differently from baseline (off-axis, higher/lower on table, different limb angles) introduces precision error from geometric mismatch. The follow-up bone area and ROI placement may differ from baseline, producing a measured 'change' that reflects positioning rather than true biological change. ISCD and manufacturer protocols emphasize exact baseline reproduction.

front: In fan-beam geometry, object centering is critical because:
back:
Off-center objects may be magnified or distorted due to the diverging beam

Fan-beam geometry uses a diverging X-ray beam, so objects near the central ray project at their true size but objects toward the edge of the field are magnified due to geometric projection. Off-center positioning distorts area calculations and therefore areal BMD. Patient centering is critical for fan-beam acquisition. Pencil-beam geometry does not have this magnification issue.
source: Standard DXA physics; manufacturer fan-beam guides

front: According to ISCD, the preferred format for reporting T-scores is:
back:
T-score, Z-score (capital, hyphenated)

ISCD reporting standards specify capital T and Z, hyphenated to 'score': T-score and Z-score. This nomenclature standardizes documentation across the field. Lowercase or unhyphenated forms are not recommended. The standardization extends to other terms: 'DXA' (not DEXA), 'BMD' (not B.M.D.), and decimal place conventions.

topic: QC failure - repeat then call service
front: What action should be taken if daily QC phantom results fall outside the manufacturer's pass/fail criteria?
back:
Repeat QC; if it fails again, call service before scanning patients

QC failure requires first repositioning the phantom and repeating the scan to rule out operator error. If the repeat scan also fails, do not scan patients; contact manufacturer service for evaluation and recalibration. Manual adjustment of QC values is never acceptable and would mask true scanner instability. The phantom itself is rarely the problem.
source: ISCD QC standards; manufacturer service protocols

front: A patient asks if their new bone pain is due to osteoporosis. The technologist should know that uncomplicated osteoporosis is:
back:
Asymptomatic until a fracture occurs

Uncomplicated osteoporosis is asymptomatic; bone loss itself causes no pain. Pain occurs only when fractures occur (especially vertebral compression, hip, or wrist fractures). New bone pain in an osteoporotic patient should prompt evaluation for fracture rather than being attributed to osteoporosis itself. Joint swelling suggests arthritis; fever suggests infection or malignancy.
source: BHOF 2022 Clinician's Guide; standard osteoporosis references

front: Under the Bone Mass Measurement Act, Medicare reimburses DXA for patients taking prolonged doses of which medication?
back:
Glucocorticoids (prolonged systemic therapy)

The BMMA explicitly covers DXA for patients on prolonged glucocorticoid therapy (typically 5 mg prednisone equivalent per day for 3+ months), one of five recognized indications. Other covered indications: estrogen-deficient women at clinical risk, vertebral abnormalities on imaging, primary hyperparathyroidism, and monitoring of FDA-approved osteoporosis therapy.
source: Medicare BMMA regulations; CMS coverage guidelines

front: The term 'osteomalacia' literally translates to:
back:
Soft bones (defective mineralization)

Osteomalacia (Greek: osteo = bone, malakia = softness) literally means 'soft bones,' referring to defective mineralization of the bone matrix despite preserved organic content. Vitamin D deficiency is the classic cause. Osteoporosis ('porous bones') is loss of bone mass with normal mineralization. These conditions are biochemically and pathophysiologically distinct.

front: When a DXA operator maintains the maximum practical workstation-to-scanner distance, which radiation safety principle is employed?
back:
Distance from the radiation source

The three cardinal principles of radiation safety are time, distance, and shielding. Increasing distance from the source exponentially decreases exposure via the inverse-square law (dose at 2 m is 1/4 of dose at 1 m). DXA operators typically stand 1-2 meters from the scanner, well outside the primary beam, where scatter dose is minimal.

front: How does a history of a laminectomy affect the DXA procedure?
back:
It removes posterior bone elements, resulting in a falsely lower bone mass reading in the ROI

Laminectomy removes the lamina and possibly the spinous process at the affected level, reducing bone in the DXA ROI. This falsely lowers BMC and BMD at the affected vertebra. Per ISCD 2023, the affected vertebra must be excluded from analysis. The unaffected vertebrae provide valid diagnostic information (minimum 2 evaluable contiguous vertebrae required).

front: Which region of interest is typically the most difficult to achieve high precision in due to its reliance on strict internal rotation?
back:
Proximal femur (femoral neck)

The femoral neck is the most precision-sensitive ROI in DXA because its 2D projection changes substantially with even small rotational differences. Variation in internal rotation between baseline and follow-up produces measured BMD changes that reflect positioning rather than biological change. This is why the total hip (broader ROI, less rotation-sensitive) is often preferred for monitoring.

front: According to ISCD, follow-up BMD testing should be undertaken:
back:
When results are likely to influence patient management

ISCD 2023 emphasizes that follow-up DXA should only be performed when results will likely influence clinical decision-making (e.g., to assess response to therapy, monitor in high-risk untreated patients, or document expected change after treatment cessation). Routine annual scanning of all patients is not supported. The Medicare interval of 23 months reflects this principle.

front: According to 2022 ARRT specifications, what clinical indication is listed for measuring the 33% radius?
back:
Hyperparathyroidism or when central sites are invalid

ARRT 2022 BD Content Specifications and ISCD 2023 list the 33% radius indications as: primary hyperparathyroidism (cortical bone loss), and as an alternative diagnostic site when the spine and hips are invalid (severe artifact, hardware, kyphosis, weight limit). The 33% radius is approximately 95% cortical, making it ideal for these uses.
source: ARRT 2022 BD Content Specifications; ISCD 2023 forearm positions

front: What positioning aid is utilized during a lumbar spine DXA to separate the intervertebral spaces?
back:
Large leg block under the knees

A large leg block (foam wedge) is placed under the knees to flex the hips, which flattens the natural lumbar lordosis. This brings the vertebral bodies parallel to the scan table and opens the intervertebral disc spaces for accurate ROI placement and marker positioning. Without the leg block, lordosis causes overlapping vertebrae on PA projection.
source: ISCD 2023 spine acquisition; manufacturer positioning guides

front: A patient presents with a T-score of -1.5 but has suffered a low-trauma hip fracture. What is their clinical diagnosis?
back:
Clinical osteoporosis based on fragility fracture

Per NBHA 2014 and BHOF, a low-trauma (fragility) hip fracture is diagnostic of clinical osteoporosis regardless of T-score. Hip fractures specifically meet the criterion alone (other sites need BMD context). This patient with a T-score of -1.5 would be labeled osteopenia by BMD alone, but the hip fracture upgrades the diagnosis to clinical osteoporosis, warranting bone-protective treatment.

front: Which component of the DXA system specifically shapes the X-ray beam?
back:
The collimator at the tube exit

The collimator is a lead aperture at the X-ray tube exit that shapes and restricts the beam to the required geometry (pencil-beam pinhole or fan-beam slit). Proper collimation defines the scan field and minimizes scatter. The anode generates X-rays. The k-edge filter splits the beam into dual energy peaks (in GE systems). The detector records attenuation.
source: Standard radiologic physics; DXA system architecture

front: What is the primary function of the 'tissue subtraction' algorithm?
back:
To mathematically eliminate soft tissue attenuation to isolate bone mass

Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry uses two photon energies because bone and soft tissue attenuate them differently. The tissue subtraction algorithm solves the dual-energy attenuation equations at each pixel, mathematically removing the soft-tissue contribution and isolating bone mineral. Soft-tissue-only pixels (around the bone) provide the reference baseline for this subtraction.
source: Standard DXA physics; dual-energy absorptiometry theory

front: Which is a recognized clinical indication for Medicare reimbursement of a DXA scan under the BMMA?
back:
Estrogen-deficient women at clinical risk for osteoporosis

The Bone Mass Measurement Act (BMMA) covers DXA for five specific indications: (1) estrogen-deficient women at clinical risk, (2) individuals with vertebral abnormalities on imaging, (3) individuals on prolonged glucocorticoid therapy, (4) primary hyperparathyroidism, and (5) monitoring of FDA-approved osteoporosis therapy. Routine population screening is not covered; pregnancy is a contraindication.
source: Medicare BMMA regulations; CMS coverage criteria

front: Which bone is evaluated when hyperparathyroidism is suspected, specifically because it contains a high percentage of cortical bone?
back:
33% radius of the non-dominant forearm

The 33% radius is approximately 95% cortical bone, making it uniquely sensitive to the cortical bone loss caused by primary hyperparathyroidism. Chronic PTH excess preferentially depletes cortical bone via continuous receptor activation (catabolic effect). ISCD specifically recommends the 33% radius for PHPT assessment and monitoring.
source: ISCD 2023 forearm positions; Bilezikian 2022 PHPT Fifth Workshop

front: If a patient's baseline scan was acquired on a GE machine, and their follow-up is on a Hologic machine, can absolute BMD values be directly compared?
back:
No, absolute BMD values vary significantly between manufacturers due to different calibration and algorithms

Different DXA manufacturers (Hologic, GE Lunar, Norland) use different X-ray energies, calibration standards, and bone-edge detection algorithms, producing systematically different absolute BMD values for the same patient. Direct comparison is invalid without cross-calibration. ISCD requires 30-patient cross-calibration when switching manufacturers. Standardized BMD (sBMD) is an attempt to reconcile values but is not universal.

front: A VFA scan images from:
back:
T4 to L4 spinal levels

VFA acquires from approximately T4 through L4. Upper thoracic vertebrae (T1-T3) are typically obscured by the scapulae and shoulders, making them unreliable for fracture assessment on VFA. The clinically important osteoporotic fracture zone is T7-L4 (with peaks at T7-T8 and T12-L1 in the bimodal distribution of vertebral fractures).

front: According to ISCD, TBS calculation should use which vertebrae?
back:
L1-L4 without exclusions, even with moderate degenerative changes

Per ISCD 2023, Trabecular Bone Score (TBS) is calculated using L1-L4 without exclusions, even in the presence of moderate degenerative changes. This differs from BMD analysis, which excludes vertebrae with osteophytes, fractures, or artifacts. TBS algorithms are designed to be more robust to such changes because they evaluate pixel-gray-level variations rather than direct attenuation.

front: What is the standard diagnostic ROI in a lumbar spine DXA?
back:
L1-L4 (all four lumbar vertebrae)

L1-L4 is the ISCD standard diagnostic ROI for lumbar spine DXA. All four lumbar vertebrae are included, with individual vertebrae excluded only when they show artifacts, fractures, surgical changes, or T-score discrepancy greater than 1.0 from adjacent vertebrae. T12 and L5 are not typically included; L5 is often partially obscured by the iliac crest.
source: ISCD 2023 Adult Official Positions, spine acquisition

front: The WHO defines osteoporosis as a T-score of:
back:
-2.5 or lower

WHO 1994 criteria define osteoporosis as a T-score of -2.5 or lower at the femoral neck, total hip, lumbar spine, or 33% radius. The T-score range 0 to -1.0 is normal, -1.0 to -2.5 is osteopenia (low bone mass), and -2.5 or lower is osteoporosis. Severe osteoporosis adds the criterion of at least one fragility fracture.

front: Which is considered a primary lifestyle intervention in the management of osteoporosis?
back:
Fall prevention strategies

Fall prevention is a cornerstone of osteoporosis management because the vast majority of osteoporotic fractures (hip, wrist) occur from low-trauma falls. Strategies include vision correction, balance and strength training, medication review (to reduce falls-risk drugs), home hazard assessment, and assistive devices. ISCD and BHOF identify fall prevention alongside calcium, vitamin D, and pharmacotherapy.

front: When reporting DXA results per ISCD guidelines, what is the preferred formatting for T-score?
back:
Capital T, hyphenated (T-score)

ISCD 2023 nomenclature standards specify capitalized, hyphenated T-score and Z-score. This standardization promotes consistency in clinical documentation, research, and reports. The same conventions apply to other DXA acronyms: 'DXA' (not DEXA), 'BMD' (not B.M.D.), and 'VFA' (not V.F.A.).

front: To how many decimal places should Area be reported according to ISCD?
back:
2 decimal digits

ISCD 2023 reporting standards: Area is reported to 2 decimal places (cm2). BMC is also 2 decimals (g). BMD is 3 decimals (g/cm2). T- and Z-scores are 1 decimal. Percent reference values are integers. These conventions ensure consistent precision in reports without overstating measurement accuracy.
source: ISCD 2023 Adult Official Positions, reporting standards

front: What unit of measurement is utilized for Areal Bone Mineral Density (BMD)?
back:
Grams per square centimeter (g/cm2)

Areal BMD is reported in grams per square centimeter (g/cm2), reflecting that DXA measures projected 2D bone mineral content divided by projected 2D area. True volumetric density (g/cm3) requires 3D imaging like QCT. BMC alone is reported in grams. The areal nature of DXA underestimates true density in smaller bones (children, short adults).
source: Standard DXA physics; ISCD reporting standards

front: What unit of measurement is utilized for Bone Mineral Content (BMC)?
back:
Grams (g) of absolute mass

BMC is the absolute mass of mineral in the scanned region, reported in grams. Area is reported in cm2. Areal BMD = BMC / Area is reported in g/cm2. Per ISCD 2023, BMC is reported to 2 decimal places (e.g., 31.76 g), Area to 2 decimals (cm2), and BMD to 3 decimals (g/cm2).

front: A DXA scan is ordered for a patient receiving prolonged glucocorticoid therapy. According to Medicare guidelines, this is:
back:
A standard covered indication for DXA testing

Prolonged glucocorticoid therapy (5 mg prednisone equivalent per day for 3+ months) is one of the five BMMA-covered indications for Medicare DXA reimbursement. Glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis is a major secondary cause of bone loss and warrants both baseline and follow-up DXA assessment. The BMMA's 23-month follow-up interval applies.
source: Medicare BMMA regulations; ACR GIO guidelines

front: Which specialized cells are responsible for the formation of new bone tissue?
back:
Osteoblasts (bone-forming cells)

Osteoblasts are bone-forming cells that synthesize and secrete osteoid (collagen matrix) which subsequently mineralizes into bone. Osteocytes are former osteoblasts embedded in mineralized matrix; they serve as mechanosensors. Osteoclasts resorb bone. Chondrocytes form cartilage, not bone. The osteoblast-osteoclast balance determines whether bone mass increases (formation greater than resorption) or decreases (resorption greater than formation).

front: Proper technologist ergonomics during patient transfer include:
back:
Maintaining proper body alignment and using leg strength

Proper ergonomics for patient transfer: maintain neutral spine alignment, bend at the knees (not the back), use leg muscles for lifting force, keep the load close to the body, and avoid twisting. Improper technique (back-bending, arm-only lifting, torso twisting) leads to musculoskeletal injuries. Mechanical aids (lifts, transfer boards) should be used for heavier patients.
source: OSHA ergonomics standards; ARRT 2022 BD Content Specifications

front: Undigested calcium pills in the GI tract overlying the lumbar spine will:
back:
Artificially elevate the BMD measurement

Calcium carbonate or calcium citrate pills are highly radiopaque (essentially concentrated calcium, like bone mineral). Undigested pills in the colon or stomach overlying the lumbar spine attenuate X-rays at rates the algorithm interprets as additional bone mineral, falsely elevating BMC and BMD. Patient instruction to skip calcium supplements on the morning of the scan prevents this artifact.
source: Manufacturer pre-scan protocols; standard artifact recognition

topic: Pediatric diagnosis - both fracture and Z-score
front: According to ISCD pediatric positions, can osteoporosis be diagnosed solely based on a DXA Z-score of -2.5?
back:
No, diagnosis requires both a low Z-score and a clinically significant fracture history

Per ISCD 2019 Pediatric Positions, osteoporosis cannot be diagnosed in children by Z-score alone. Diagnosis requires BOTH a low BMD (Z-score of -2.0 or lower, traditionally) AND a clinically significant fracture history (two or more long bone fractures by age 10, three by age 19, or any vertebral compression fracture without major trauma). Without fracture history, the term is 'low bone density for chronologic age'.

front: The 2022 ARRT content specifications include which new topic under Image Production?
back:
File and database management

The ARRT 2022 BD Content Specifications added file and database management to the Image Production domain. This encompasses local backup, network/PACS archiving, retrieval of prior studies for follow-up comparison, and protection of patient data. The 2027 ARRT update further renamed this section to 'Informatics' to reflect modern digital workflows.

front: What is the primary difference between accuracy and precision in DXA?
back:
Precision is reproducibility; accuracy is trueness to a standard

Precision is reproducibility: how closely repeated measurements of the same object agree. Accuracy is trueness: how close the measurement is to the actual value (e.g., ash weight of the bone). DXA has excellent precision (1-2% CV at the spine) but only moderate accuracy (5-10% error vs ash weight). For serial monitoring, precision matters most (changes within the same patient).

front: When a DXA department replaces equipment, what process was historically required but removed from the 2027 ARRT specifications?
back:
Cross-calibration between scanners

The 2027 ARRT BD Content Specifications removed cross-calibration from the entry-level technologist requirements (it remains an ISCD-recommended practice for monitoring, but is no longer a tested ARRT competency). QC phantom scanning, LSC calculation, and baseline re-establishment remain core requirements. Cross-calibration may still be performed at facilities switching equipment, just not tested.

front: What is the primary function of daily Quality Control (QC) phantom scans?
back:
Confirm scanner function, stability, and detect shift or drift

Daily phantom QC monitors scanner mechanical and electronic stability, detecting calibration shifts (sudden changes), drifts (gradual changes), and equipment failures before they affect patient scans. Results are plotted on Shewhart control charts; values outside the 1.5% control limit, or patterns of shift (10+ consecutive points on one side) or trend (5+ consecutive in one direction), trigger investigation.
source: ISCD QC standards; Lewiecki 2016 J Clin Densitom

front: Which standard score compares a patient's BMD to the mean of a healthy, young-adult reference population?
back:
T-score for young-adult comparison

T-score compares the patient's BMD to the mean of a young-adult (peak bone mass) reference population, expressed in standard deviations. T-scores are used for diagnosis in postmenopausal women and men aged 50 and older per WHO 1994 / ISCD 2023. Z-score compares to age-matched peers and is used for premenopausal women, men under 50, and children.

front: Which standard score compares a patient's BMD to an age-, sex-, and ethnicity-matched reference population?
back:
Z-score for demographic peer comparison

Z-score compares the patient's BMD to age-, sex-, and ethnicity-matched peers, expressed in standard deviations. Z-scores are used in premenopausal women, men under 50, and children where comparison to young-adult peak bone mass (T-score) is inappropriate. ISCD defines Z-score of -2.0 or lower as 'below the expected range for age'.
source: ISCD 2023 Adult Official Positions; ISCD 2019 Pediatric

front: What action should be taken if L2 is fractured and L1, L3, L4 are normal?
back:
Exclude L2 and report the average of L1, L3, and L4

A fractured vertebra (any compression fracture, particularly if it produces a T-score discrepancy greater than 1.0 from adjacent vertebrae) must be excluded from the spine BMD calculation. With L2 excluded, the remaining L1, L3, and L4 (still 3 valid vertebrae) are averaged. Per ISCD 2023, at least 2 evaluable contiguous vertebrae are required; 3 vertebrae easily meets this threshold.

front: A baseline phantom establishes:
back:
A reference mean and standard deviation for daily QC comparisons

The baseline phantom scans (typically 25 consecutive days, or 10 same-day scans depending on manufacturer protocol) establish a reference mean BMD and standard deviation for the scanner. Future daily QC scans are compared to this baseline. Deviations from the baseline mean indicate scanner drift, shift, or failure requiring investigation or service.
source: ISCD QC standards; manufacturer baseline protocols

front: A key advantage of dual photon absorptiometry is:
back:
Ability to correct for soft-tissue variations surrounding the bone

Dual photon absorptiometry (and its modern X-ray successor, DXA) uses two distinct photon energies to mathematically separate bone from surrounding soft tissue. The differential attenuation of high and low energies through bone versus soft tissue allows pixel-by-pixel subtraction of soft tissue contribution. Single-energy methods cannot correct for variable soft-tissue thickness around the bone.

front: Which patient demographic is at the highest statistical risk for developing osteoporosis?
back:
Caucasian and Asian postmenopausal women

Caucasian and Asian postmenopausal women have the highest osteoporosis prevalence due to a combination of lower peak bone mass, smaller bone size, and rapid post-menopausal bone loss from estrogen withdrawal. African American women achieve higher peak bone mass and have lower fracture rates. Men have larger bone size and more gradual sex hormone decline, conferring lower risk than women of the same ethnicity.
source: BHOF 2022 Clinician's Guide; NHANES III epidemiology

front: The WHO defines normal bone density as a T-score of:
back:
-1.0 or higher

WHO 1994 classifies T-score of -1.0 or higher as normal. T-score -1.0 to -2.5 is osteopenia (low bone mass). T-score of -2.5 or lower is osteoporosis. T-score of -2.5 or lower plus fragility fracture is severe (established) osteoporosis. The thresholds apply to postmenopausal women and men aged 50+; Z-scores apply to younger populations.

front: A patient with a BMI of 40 exceeds the weight limit for the DXA table. What is the most appropriate alternative?
back:
Scan the non-dominant forearm seated off-table

When a patient exceeds the manufacturer's table weight limit (typically 350-450 lb depending on scanner model), the forearm 33% radius can be scanned with the patient seated beside the table. The forearm is an ISCD-approved diagnostic site. QUS is not diagnostic per ISCD. Manual table support is unsafe. Standard X-ray cannot quantify BMD.
source: ISCD 2023 alternative-site positions; manufacturer weight limits

front: What occurs during the 'tissue subtraction' phase of analysis?
back:
High and low energy attenuation data are used to mathematically eliminate soft tissue

DXA's dual-energy subtraction uses the differential attenuation of high (typically 70 or 100 kVp) and low (40 kVp range) photon energies through bone and soft tissue. Bone attenuates low-energy photons disproportionately, while soft tissue has a more linear attenuation across energies. Solving the dual-energy equations pixel-by-pixel mathematically isolates bone mineral from soft tissue.

front: The term 'discordance' in bone densitometry refers to:
back:
Differing WHO classifications between two skeletal sites in the same patient

Discordance occurs when a patient's BMD measurements at different anatomical sites fall into different WHO diagnostic categories (e.g., osteoporosis at spine, osteopenia at hip). Per ISCD 2023, diagnosis follows the lowest T-score from standard sites. Discordance can reflect true site-specific bone loss patterns (spine vs hip respond differently to therapy) or artifact at one site.

front: When scanning a patient for a VFA, the image is typically acquired in which projection?
back:
Lateral (side-on) projection

VFA uses lateral imaging to visualize vertebral body height and shape, required for Genant semi-quantitative grading of compression fractures. PA spine DXA cannot assess vertebral morphology; only lateral imaging shows the wedge, biconcave, or crush morphology that defines fractures. The patient is positioned in the lateral decubitus position with arms forward.

front: Which of the following is considered an antiresorptive medication?
back:
Alendronate (oral bisphosphonate)

Alendronate (Fosamax) is an oral nitrogen-containing bisphosphonate that inhibits osteoclast farnesyl pyrophosphate synthase, reducing bone resorption. It is classed as a pure antiresorptive. Teriparatide is anabolic (stimulates formation). Romosozumab is dual-action (anabolic via sclerostin inhibition, with antiresorptive effects). Calcium is a supplement, not a drug class.

front: What effect does extreme obesity typically have on the accuracy of a DXA scan?
back:
It creates beam hardening artifacts that artificially alter BMD

Excessive soft tissue thickness causes beam hardening: lower-energy photons are absorbed preferentially, shifting the beam's effective energy higher. This skews the high-to-low energy attenuation ratio used in the dual-energy BMD calculation, producing systematic over- or under-estimation depending on the manufacturer's algorithm. Bariatric protocols and thicker scan modes attempt to compensate.
source: Standard DXA physics; ISCD body habitus considerations

front: A Z-score of -1.8 in a 35-year-old premenopausal woman indicates:
back:
Within the expected range for her age

In premenopausal women, T-scores and WHO classifications (osteopenia/osteoporosis) do not apply. Z-scores compare to age-matched peers. A Z-score above -2.0 is termed 'within the expected range for age'; Z-score of -2.0 or lower is 'below the expected range for age'. A Z-score of -1.8 is within the expected range, though it does not preclude clinical fragility if other risk factors are present.
source: ISCD 2023 Adult Official Positions, premenopausal reporting

front: Which skeletal site is generally preferred for monitoring the efficacy of osteoporosis treatment?
back:
Lumbar spine (highly responsive trabecular bone)

The lumbar spine is approximately 66% trabecular bone and is metabolically active, responding faster and more measurably to antiresorptive or anabolic therapy than cortical-rich sites. Treatment-induced BMD changes are typically larger at the spine than the hip. ISCD recommends the spine as the primary monitoring site, with the hip used when the spine is invalid due to artifact or surgery.

front: During a femur scan, lack of proper internal rotation causes the lesser trochanter to appear:
back:
Prominent and enlarged on the image

Proper internal rotation of 15-25 degrees hides the lesser trochanter posteriorly. Under-rotation (less than 15 degrees, or external rotation) causes the lesser trochanter to project outward and appear prominent and enlarged. This is the key visual cue that positioning is inadequate. A foreshortened femoral neck and falsely elevated BMD accompany this finding.
source: ISCD 2023 hip acquisition; manufacturer positioning guides

front: Severe scoliosis of the lumbar spine affects the DXA scan by:
back:
Causing uneven ROI placement and falsely altering BMD results

Severe scoliosis (Cobb angle greater than 15-20 degrees) tilts vertebrae so horizontal intervertebral lines cannot be cleanly placed. ROI boxes cannot align consistently across vertebrae, distorting area calculations and corrupting areal BMD. Mild scoliosis may still allow valid spine analysis with careful ROI placement; severe scoliosis typically requires hip or forearm reliance.
source: ISCD 2023 spine analysis; manufacturer scoliosis guidance

front: What is the biological half-life of most bisphosphonates in human bone?
back:
Greater than 10 years in skeletal tissue

Bisphosphonates bind strongly to hydroxyapatite in bone mineral and can persist in the skeleton for over 10 years after the last dose. This long skeletal half-life underlies the 'drug holiday' concept after 3-5 years of bisphosphonate therapy in low-risk patients: the drug continues to suppress osteoclast activity after dosing stops. Denosumab in contrast has no skeletal retention and requires uninterrupted dosing.
source: FDA bisphosphonate labels; ASBMR drug holiday position

front: In DXA physics, 'attenuation' refers to:
back:
Reduction in intensity of the X-ray beam as it passes through matter

Attenuation is the reduction in X-ray beam intensity as photons are absorbed (photoelectric effect, dominant at low energies) or scattered (Compton scatter, dominant at higher energies) by matter. Dense materials like bone attenuate more than soft tissue; low-energy photons attenuate more than high-energy. DXA exploits this energy-dependent attenuation difference to separate bone from soft tissue.

front: If a technologist discovers the phantom was placed off-center during daily QC, what should they do?
back:
Reposition the phantom correctly and repeat the QC scan

An off-center phantom introduces operator-side error that does not reflect scanner stability. The correct response is to reposition according to manufacturer specifications (ensuring proper centering and orientation) and repeat the scan. This isolates true scanner performance from positioning artifacts. Accepting off-center QC results risks missing true drift or shift in scanner calibration.
source: Manufacturer QC protocols; ISCD QC standards

front: When evaluating pediatric patients, the ISCD recommends using which reference database?
back:
Age, sex, and ethnicity-matched pediatric reference data

Pediatric DXA requires age-, sex-, and ethnicity-matched pediatric reference databases because growing skeletons cannot be compared to adult peak bone mass references (T-scores). Manufacturers provide validated pediatric reference databases. Bone size adjustments (BMAD or height-for-age Z-score corrections) may be appropriate in short or tall children to avoid systematic over- or under-estimation.

front: What is the primary advantage of utilizing DXA over Quantitative Computed Tomography (QCT)?
back:
DXA exposes the patient to a significantly lower radiation dose

DXA delivers approximately 1-10 microSv per scan site, while QCT delivers approximately 50-1500 microSv depending on protocol. This 10-150x dose advantage makes DXA the preferred screening and monitoring tool. QCT does provide true volumetric BMD and can separate cortical from trabecular bone, but those advantages do not outweigh DXA's substantial dose, cost, and availability benefits.
source: ISCD radiation safety white paper; ICRP recommendations

front: According to the 2027 ARRT updates, what functional tool must technologists understand to optimize VFA image visualization?
back:
Window level (contrast and brightness)

Window leveling (adjusting display contrast and brightness) optimizes visualization of vertebral endplates and morphology for Genant semi-quantitative grading on VFA images. The 2027 ARRT BD update added window level as a Procedures competency. Proper window leveling reveals subtle Grade 1 (mild, 20-25% height loss) fractures that might be missed with default display settings.
source: ARRT 2027 BD Content Specifications; ISCD VFA positions

topic: Spine ROI landmarks T12 iliac crests
front: When acquiring a lumbar spine DXA, which bony landmarks verify the correct ROI?
back:
Iliac crests and lowest pair of ribs (T12)

Identification of T12 (lowest pair of ribs articulate here) and L4-L5 (at the level of the iliac crests in most patients, though varying with anatomy) ensures accurate vertebral labeling. In 70-86% of patients, the iliac crest crosses at L4-L5. Mislabeling vertebrae produces systematically incorrect diagnosis. Landmark verification is part of standard spine DXA protocol.
source: ISCD 2023 spine acquisition; manufacturer labeling guides

front: In proximal femur scanning, what is the required femoral neck rotation?
back:
15-25 degrees of internal rotation

Internal rotation of 15-25 degrees brings the femoral neck parallel to the scan table, producing the longest neck projection and hiding the lesser trochanter posteriorly. The hip positioner secures the foot in this position. Inadequate rotation causes prominent lesser trochanter and foreshortened femoral neck, producing falsely elevated BMD.

front: Which ROI is the ONLY recommended diagnostic site in the forearm according to ISCD?
back:
33% (one-third) radius (predominantly cortical)

The 33% radius is the only ISCD-recognized diagnostic forearm ROI. It is approximately 95% cortical bone, making it especially sensitive to cortical bone loss (primary hyperparathyroidism, thyrotoxicosis, hyperthyroidism). The ultradistal radius is largely trabecular but is not used for WHO diagnosis. The mid-distal radius is reported but not for diagnosis.

front: Vertebral Fracture Assessment (VFA) is designed to:
back:
Detect vertebral compression fractures primarily between T7-L4

VFA images T4 through L4 to detect vertebral compression fractures, particularly in the clinically important T7-L4 zone where 90% of osteoporotic vertebral fractures occur. The bimodal distribution peaks at T7-T8 (mid-thoracic, from anterior wedge loading) and T12-L1 (thoracolumbar junction). VFA uses Genant semi-quantitative grading and complements BMD assessment.

front: According to ISCD, what clinical finding indicates the need for a follow-up VFA?
back:
Historical height loss greater than 4 cm or 1.5 inches

Historical height loss greater than 4 cm (1.5 inches) from peak height is a strong indication for VFA, since unrecognized vertebral compression fractures reduce stature. Measured height loss greater than 2 cm during DXA monitoring (current vs prior documented) also prompts VFA. Other VFA indications include age 70+ with T-score less than -1.0, glucocorticoid therapy, and self-reported but undocumented prior vertebral fracture.

front: The standard clinical tool to systematically grade vertebral fractures visually on VFA is:
back:
Genant's semi-quantitative grading scheme

Genant's semi-quantitative grading is the standard for visual VFA fracture assessment. Grade 0 = normal (less than 20% height loss), Grade 1 = mild (20-25%), Grade 2 = moderate (25-40%), Grade 3 = severe (greater than 40%). Heights are measured at anterior, middle, and posterior of each vertebral body and compared to adjacent normal vertebrae. FRAX and TBS are separate tools for risk and microarchitecture.

front: According to the WHO criteria, the diagnosis of osteoporosis based on the measurement of bone density is:
back:
2.5 SDs or more below the young adult peak bone density

WHO 1994 defines osteoporosis as a T-score of -2.5 or lower at the femoral neck, total hip, lumbar spine, or 33% radius. This corresponds to 2.5 standard deviations or more below young adult peak bone mass. Severe (established) osteoporosis adds one or more fragility fractures to that BMD threshold.

front: Which is the current gold standard technique for measuring bone density?
back:
DXA (dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry)

DXA is the universally accepted gold standard for bone density measurement and the basis for WHO diagnostic criteria. It offers the best balance of precision, low radiation dose, cost, and reproducibility. DPA is obsolete (replaced by DXA). QCT provides true volumetric density but with higher dose. QUS is screening only and not diagnostic per ISCD.

front: Cancellous bone is also known as:
back:
Trabecular bone with a spongy structure

Cancellous bone and trabecular bone are synonymous terms for the spongy honeycomb-like internal bone network found in vertebrae and the ends of long bones. It makes up about 20 percent of skeletal mass but has high surface area and metabolic activity. Cortical (compact) bone is the dense outer shell. Osteocytes are bone cells, not a bone type.

front: What anatomical landmark on the proximal femur indicates the degree of rotation of the proximal femur?
back:
Lesser trochanter visibility

The lesser trochanter is the most reliable visual indicator of femur rotation. With proper 15-25 degrees of internal rotation it appears small or hidden behind the femoral shaft. A prominent, enlarged lesser trochanter signals inadequate internal rotation and a foreshortened femoral neck, both of which falsely elevate BMD.
source: ISCD 2023 hip acquisition; manufacturer positioning guides

front: Which lumbar vertebra is described as having a characteristic shape of an X or H on a DXA PA spine scan?
back:
L4 (largest, with characteristic X or H shape)

L4 has a distinctive X or H shape on PA DXA images due to its broad transverse processes and the visualization of the iliac crests at its level. This characteristic morphology helps confirm correct vertebral labeling. The iliac crest typically aligns with L4-L5, providing the anatomical reference for identifying L4.
source: Manufacturer spine identification guides; standard DXA reference

front: Which of the following is NOT a bisphosphonate?
back:
Forteo (teriparatide, an anabolic PTH analog)

Forteo (teriparatide) is recombinant human PTH(1-34), an anabolic agent that stimulates bone formation. It is NOT a bisphosphonate. Alendronate, etidronate, and zoledronic acid are all bisphosphonates that bind hydroxyapatite and inhibit osteoclast resorption. The 24-month treatment cap and osteosarcoma boxed warning on Forteo were both removed by FDA in November 2020.
source: FDA drug labels; ASBMR drug classification

front: All of the following statements are true about QCT EXCEPT:
back:
Results are usually reported in g/cm squared

QCT measures true volumetric density (g/cm cubed), NOT areal density (g/cm squared). Areal density is the DXA unit. QCT advantages include true 3D measurement, separate cortical/trabecular analysis, and avoidance of overlapping structure artifacts. QCT disadvantages include higher dose and marrow fat causing apparent BMD underestimation in elderly patients.
source: Standard QCT physics; ISCD QCT positions

front: Peripheral BMD measurements are advocated for:
back:
Screening when central DXA is unavailable

Peripheral DXA (pDXA, scanning forearm or heel with dedicated peripheral devices) is positioned as a screening tool when central DXA is not readily available, such as in primary care offices. Per ISCD 2023, pDXA is not used for diagnosis (only central DXA is diagnostic per WHO criteria) but can identify patients who should proceed to central DXA.

front: What is the most important clinical consequence of osteoporosis?
back:
Fracture (the defining clinical outcome)

Fracture is the defining clinical consequence of osteoporosis: hip fractures cause significant morbidity and mortality (20-25% one-year mortality in older adults), vertebral fractures cause pain, kyphosis, and restrictive lung disease, and wrist fractures cause functional impairment. Height loss and shape change are secondary to vertebral fractures. Low bone mass alone without fracture is asymptomatic.
source: BHOF 2022 Clinician's Guide; standard osteoporosis references

front: What is the name of the tool that integrates clinical information in a quantitative manner to predict a 10-year probability of major osteoporotic fracture for both men and women?
back:
FRAX (Fracture Risk Assessment Tool)

FRAX (Fracture Risk Assessment Tool) integrates 10 clinical risk factors with femoral neck BMD (or without BMD) to calculate the 10-year probability of major osteoporotic fracture and hip fracture. Validated for untreated adults aged 40-90, FRAX is country-specific and used to guide treatment decisions. TBS adjusts FRAX but does not calculate fracture probability alone.

front: What is the major source of precision error in clinical practice?
back:
The technologist's positioning consistency

The technologist's ability to consistently position the patient and place ROIs is the dominant source of precision error in clinical practice. Modern scanners have excellent intrinsic precision (less than 0.5% CV phantom); operator-side variance from patient positioning typically produces total in vivo precision of 1-2% CV. This is why in vivo precision studies (with repositioning) measure realistic clinical precision.

front: What is the approximate percentage of trabecular bone in the skeleton?
back:
20 percent of total skeletal mass

Trabecular bone comprises approximately 20 percent of total skeletal mass; cortical bone makes up the other 80 percent. Despite its small mass fraction, trabecular bone has a much higher surface-to-volume ratio and turns over about 8 times faster than cortical bone, making it the primary site of early osteoporotic loss and the most responsive to therapy.

front: The forearm is not used to monitor the effects of therapy for osteoporosis because:
back:
Rate of BMD change is too slow to detect therapy response

The 33% radius is approximately 95% cortical bone, and cortical bone turns over slowly (about 3% per year vs 25% per year for trabecular bone). Therapy-induced BMD changes at the cortical forearm are small and slow, making the site insensitive for monitoring treatment response. The trabecular-rich spine is preferred for monitoring; the forearm is used for diagnosis (PHPT, central site alternatives).
source: ISCD 2023 monitoring positions; standard bone biology

front: Which of the following does NOT help in achieving peak bone mass?
back:
Long-term corticosteroid therapy

Long-term corticosteroid (glucocorticoid) therapy is the most common cause of drug-induced bone loss and impairs peak bone mass acquisition in children and adolescents. Adequate calcium/vitamin D, weight-bearing exercise, and normal puberty (especially estrogen exposure in girls) are essential for achieving peak bone mass in the second and third decades of life.
source: BHOF 2022 Clinician's Guide; ACR GIO guidelines

front: Which of the following sites is mostly composed of cortical bone?
back:
Proximal forearm (33% radius)

The proximal forearm at the 33% radius is approximately 95% cortical bone, the highest cortical fraction among standard DXA sites. The ultradistal radius is approximately 50-66% trabecular. The femoral neck is mixed (about 50% trabecular). Vertebral bodies are approximately 66% trabecular. The 33% radius is therefore the most sensitive site for cortical-depleting diseases like primary hyperparathyroidism.
source: Standard bone composition references; ISCD forearm positions

topic: QUS heel does not require water immersion
front: Which of the following statements is FALSE concerning QUS advantages over X-ray methodologies for BMD measurement?
back:
The patient's heel must be immersed in liquid during the procedure

The FALSE statement: most modern QUS devices use dry coupling (gel or coupling pads), not water immersion. Older heel QUS devices used water baths, but contemporary clinical devices use dry coupling. The other statements are true: QUS is inexpensive, uses no ionizing radiation, and is quick. However, QUS is not diagnostic per ISCD; it is positioned as a screening tool.
source: ISCD 2023 QUS positions; manufacturer device specifications

front: What is the name of the isotope that was used in SPA (single photon absorptiometry)?
back:
Iodine-125

Single Photon Absorptiometry (SPA) used Iodine-125 (I-125) as the radioisotope source, providing monoenergetic photons for forearm scanning. SPA could not correct for soft-tissue variations and was largely replaced by SXA and then DXA. DPA (dual photon absorptiometry) used Gadolinium-153 (Gd-153), which provided two photon energies. Both technologies are now obsolete.

front: What is the texture index that evaluates the pixel gray-level variations in lumbar spine DXA images?
back:
TBS (Trabecular Bone Score)

Trabecular Bone Score (TBS) is a textural index derived from the lumbar spine DXA image by analyzing pixel gray-level variations to estimate bone microarchitecture quality. TBS is independent of areal BMD and adds value in fracture risk prediction, particularly in T2DM and glucocorticoid-treated patients. ISCD 2023 endorses TBS use in adults aged 40+ within the manufacturer's BMI validity range.

front: Type 2 primary osteoporosis occurs:
back:
Generally after age 70 in both sexes (senile osteoporosis)

Type 2 primary osteoporosis (senile osteoporosis) occurs generally after age 70 in both men and women, related to age-related bone loss, reduced osteoblast function, and calcium/vitamin D insufficiency. Type 1 primary (postmenopausal osteoporosis) is estrogen-deficiency related in postmenopausal women. Medication-induced bone loss is classified as secondary osteoporosis.

front: What technique uses images of the hand and an aluminum wedge?
back:
RA (radiographic absorptiometry)

Radiographic Absorptiometry (RA) uses a standard radiograph of the hand including an aluminum wedge as a calibration reference. Software analyzes the optical density of the middle phalanges of the second, third, and fourth fingers relative to the aluminum wedge to estimate BMD. RA is a legacy technique largely replaced by DXA and pDXA but is still occasionally used in screening.
source: Standard densitometry history; ISCD peripheral positions

front: New bone is formed by:
back:
Osteoblasts (bone-forming cells)

Osteoblasts synthesize and mineralize osteoid (the bone matrix), forming new bone tissue. They are derived from mesenchymal stem cells and work in coordination with osteoclasts during the remodeling cycle. Osteoclasts resorb bone. Cortical and cancellous are types of bone tissue, not cellular agents of formation.

front: Cancellous bone is also known as:
back:
Trabecular bone (the spongy internal network)

Cancellous bone and trabecular bone are synonymous terms for the spongy, honeycomb-like internal bone network found in vertebrae and the ends of long bones. Cortical (compact) bone is the dense outer shell. Approximately 20% of the skeleton is trabecular and 80% is cortical, though the metabolic activity ratio is inverted.

front: How would you start labeling the vertebral bodies on a PA lumbar scan?
back:
Start at L4 (identified by iliac crests) and count or label upward

Standard practice is to identify L4 using the iliac crests as the anatomical landmark (the crests typically align with the L4-L5 disc space) and then count upward to L1. This is more reliable than starting at L1 because identifying T12 (lowest rib-bearing vertebra) can be obscured by overlying soft tissue. Software-only labeling without verification risks mislabeling in patients with anatomical variants (4 or 6 lumbar vertebrae).
source: ISCD 2023 spine acquisition; manufacturer labeling guides

front: With changes in rotation of the femoral neck, what happens?
back:
BMD changes (typically increases with under-rotation)

Under-rotation foreshortens the femoral neck on the 2D projection, concentrating the same bone into a smaller projected area. Since BMD = BMC/Area, the smaller area inflates the measured BMD. Over-rotation also distorts the projection. Reproducible 15-25 degrees of internal rotation is essential for accurate diagnosis and valid follow-up monitoring.

front: In the United States, if the daily QC fails, what should be your first step of action?
back:
Repeat the scan after repositioning the phantom

Standard QC failure response: reposition the phantom carefully and repeat the scan. The most common cause of a single failed QC is positioning error, not scanner malfunction. If the repeat scan also fails, escalate to service before scanning patients. Scanning on a non-compliant scanner produces unreliable BMD that cannot be trusted clinically.
source: Manufacturer QC protocols; ISCD QC standards

front: According to WHO criteria, if a healthy female age 36, with no known medical condition, has a T-score of -1.6 SD, how would she be diagnosed?
back:
None of the above (WHO criteria do not apply at age 36)

WHO T-score criteria apply only to postmenopausal women and men aged 50 and older. At age 36, this premenopausal woman should be evaluated using Z-scores, not T-scores. Per ISCD, a Z-score of -2.0 or lower is 'below the expected range for age,' and a Z-score above -2.0 is 'within the expected range.' WHO osteopenia/osteoporosis labels are inappropriate at her age.

topic: DXA results differ - all of the above restructured
front: Although the technology is similar across DXA units, BMD results differ between manufacturers because of:
back:
Different calibration standards, algorithms, ROIs, and reference databases

BMD results vary significantly between manufacturers due to multiple factors: different calibration standards (Hologic CaHA phantom vs GE Aluminum Spine Phantom), different algorithms for bone edge detection and tissue subtraction, slightly different ROI definitions, and different reference databases (though NHANES III is the universal femoral neck T-score standard). This is why cross-calibration is required when switching manufacturers.

front: The primary mechanism of action of antiresorptive therapies is direct interaction with:
back:
Osteoclasts (the bone-resorbing cells)

Antiresorptive therapies (bisphosphonates, denosumab, raloxifene, calcitonin) all target osteoclasts to reduce bone resorption. Bisphosphonates inhibit osteoclast farnesyl pyrophosphate synthase; denosumab is an anti-RANKL antibody that prevents osteoclast formation; raloxifene activates estrogen receptors to suppress osteoclast activity. Anabolic agents (teriparatide, romosozumab) target osteoblasts instead.

front: Since fracture risk is best assessed in women over 60, and because degenerative artifacts can interfere with spine BMD in older women, which site is preferred for fracture prediction?
back:
Proximal femur (hip)

The proximal femur (femoral neck and total hip) is the preferred fracture-prediction site in older women because the spine is often confounded by degenerative changes (osteophytes, facet hypertrophy, vascular calcification) that falsely elevate spine BMD. The femoral neck is also the FRAX input site. Calcaneal QUS is screening only; forearm is alternative when central sites fail.

front: Which of the following diseases is NOT associated with low BMD?
back:
Osteopetrosis (rare disease of impaired osteoclast function causing HIGH BMD)

Osteopetrosis is the answer because it is a rare genetic disease of impaired osteoclast function leading to dense, brittle bones with HIGH BMD (not low). Despite high BMD, osteopetrotic bone is structurally abnormal and prone to fracture. The other options all reduce BMD: osteomalacia (defective mineralization), multiple myeloma (lytic bone lesions), and osteogenesis imperfecta (collagen defect causing low bone mass).
source: Standard medical references; metabolic bone disease

front: DXA results may be influenced by all of the following EXCEPT:
back:
The patient's preferred examination time of day

The exception is examination time of day, which has no measurable effect on DXA results. Body thickness affects beam attenuation and scan mode selection. Degenerative changes (osteophytes, facet hypertrophy) falsely elevate spine BMD. Compression fractures concentrate bone into smaller area, also falsely elevating BMD. Scan mode itself can affect results if changed between scans without cross-validation.

front: A great forearm scan should include all of the following EXCEPT:
back:
Patient motion during acquisition

Patient motion is the exception - motion is NEVER desirable in any DXA scan. A quality forearm scan requires the forearm centered, straight, pronated, and free of artifacts (jewelry, watches, removable metal). The patient must remain still throughout acquisition. Motion blurs the image, disrupts edge detection, and corrupts the BMD calculation.

front: Phantoms that mimic the shape and size of a skeletal region are called:
back:
Anthropomorphic phantoms (skeleton-shaped)

Anthropomorphic phantoms are designed to mimic the shape, size, and attenuation properties of human skeletal regions (typically the lumbar spine). Examples include the European Spine Phantom, Hologic's CaHA-in-epoxy spine phantom, and GE's aluminum spine phantom. They are used for daily QC to monitor scanner stability under realistic clinical attenuation conditions.
source: ISCD QC standards; manufacturer phantom specifications

front: If a patient's BMC is 60.58 g and the area is 61.60 cm-squared, what is her BMD?
back:
0.983 g/cm-squared

BMD = BMC / Area = 60.58 g / 61.60 cm-squared = 0.9834 g/cm-squared, which rounds to 0.983 (per ISCD reporting standard of 3 decimal places). This calculation is a fundamental DXA computation and exam staple. Recognize that BMC slightly less than Area gives BMD just below 1.0.
source: Standard DXA physics; ISCD reporting standards

front: How often should you perform the QA on the DXA unit?
back:
Every day before patient scanning

Daily QA (typically a phantom scan) is required before patient scanning to detect any overnight drift, shift, or hardware issue. Skipping daily QA risks reporting BMD on a malfunctioning scanner. Some facilities also perform weekly cross-calibration phantom checks and quarterly multi-phantom comprehensive QA, but the daily phantom scan is the minimum standard.
source: ISCD QC standards; manufacturer service requirements

front: Radiogrammetry is the measurement of bones using skeletal X-rays. Which site was often used?
back:
Metacarpals (hand bones)

Radiogrammetry uses standard radiographs to measure cortical width of the metacarpals (typically the second metacarpal) as an indicator of bone mass. It is a legacy technique from the era before DXA, now largely replaced. Digital radiogrammetry (DXR) is an updated version still used occasionally for screening or research, but DXA is the diagnostic standard.

front: For voltage-switching DXA units, what type of calibration is used?
back:
Continuous internal calibration (calibration wheel or drum)

Voltage-switching DXA systems (Hologic) use continuous internal calibration via a rotating calibration wheel or drum positioned in the beam path. This provides a real-time reference standard during patient scanning, allowing automatic correction for drift. K-edge filtration systems (GE Lunar) use a different external reference approach. Both manufacturers require daily phantom QC regardless of internal calibration mechanisms.

front: For low energies, the most common process of absorption is the:
back:
Photoelectric effect (dominant at low energies)

The photoelectric effect dominates at low X-ray energies (below approximately 50 keV) and is the primary mechanism for bone-to-soft-tissue attenuation contrast in DXA. The photoelectric absorption probability is proportional to Z-cubed (atomic number), so high-Z materials like calcium (Z=20) attenuate much more than soft tissue (effective Z about 7) at low energies. Compton dominates at higher energies; pair production requires >1.022 MeV.
source: Standard radiologic physics; DXA imaging principles

front: Which of the following would best describe the effective dose for a central DXA scan?
back:
All of the above describe DXA effective dose

Central DXA effective dose (1-10 uSv) is comparable to all three references: less than one day of background radiation (about 7 uSv/day), less than a round-trip transcontinental flight (about 50-80 uSv), and roughly 1/10 the dose of a chest X-ray (about 20 uSv). These analogies are commonly used to reassure patients about the very low DXA dose.
source: ICRP recommendations; ISCD radiation safety white paper

front: Why is the non-dominant arm used in bone densitometry?
back:
Reference values reflect non-dominant arm measurements

The non-dominant arm is used because the reference databases were established using non-dominant forearm measurements. The dominant arm is subject to mechanical loading (Wolff's law) which can elevate BMD by 3-5% versus the non-dominant side. Using the non-dominant arm provides a conservative baseline that is also reproducible for follow-up.
source: ISCD 2023 forearm positions; manufacturer reference databases

topic: Neck ROI perpendicular and both ends in bone
front: When measuring the proximal femur, which of the following statements is true?
back:
The neck ROI is perpendicular to the femoral neck axis

The femoral neck ROI is placed perpendicular to the long axis of the femoral neck, centered at its narrowest point. It must NOT include the greater trochanter or ischium. Both ends of the ROI box should sit cleanly within the neck contour. Software places an initial ROI but operator review and adjustment is standard practice, especially for variant anatomy.
