source: ISCD 2023 hip acquisition; manufacturer ROI guides

front: What is the bone edge detection methodology?
back:
It tests the machine's ability to distinguish bone from soft tissue

Bone edge detection is the software's ability to identify where bone ends and soft tissue begins in the DXA image. This delineation determines the projected bone area used in the BMD calculation (BMD = BMC/Area). Edge detection failures (poor contrast, obesity, motion) corrupt the area calculation and therefore the BMD. Operator review of edge contours is essential, particularly for low-contrast scans.
source: Standard DXA analysis; manufacturer edge detection

front: All of the following are examples of weight-bearing exercise EXCEPT:
back:
Lap swimming in a pool

Lap swimming is NOT weight-bearing because water buoyancy supports body weight, eliminating the mechanical load that stimulates bone formation. Weight-bearing exercise (running, dancing, racket sports, walking, stair climbing) loads the skeleton against gravity and is beneficial for bone health. Swimming is excellent cardiovascular exercise but does not contribute to bone mass maintenance.
source: BHOF 2022 exercise recommendations; standard bone health references

front: What are the two most common methods for creating dual energies in DXA units?
back:
K-edge filtering (GE Lunar) and rapid energy switching (Hologic)

The two standard methods for generating dual X-ray energies are: (1) K-edge filtration with a rare-earth filter (cerium in GE Lunar systems, applied to a constant 76 kVp beam to produce two distinct energy peaks), and (2) rapid voltage/energy switching between high and low kVp (Hologic uses 100/140 kVp switching). Both methods enable the dual-energy attenuation comparison needed for BMD calculation.
source: Manufacturer scanner specifications; standard DXA physics

front: Why do you use the hip positioner during scanning of the proximal femur?
back:
To place the femoral neck parallel to the tabletop

The hip positioner immobilizes the feet at 15-25 degrees of internal rotation, which brings the femoral neck parallel to the scan tabletop. This provides the longest neck projection and hides the lesser trochanter posteriorly. Ward's area position is determined by software, not by positioning. Total hip ROI is the algorithm's region; the positioner ensures consistent anatomical alignment.

front: If a patient is found to have coins in their pocket while obtaining the proximal femur, what should you do?
back:
Remove the coins and rescan the hip

Metal objects (coins, zippers, snaps, jewelry, piercings) are highly radiopaque and falsely elevate BMD in the affected region. The technologist must remove all metal items from the scan field and rescan to obtain valid measurements. Pre-scan screening and gown changing minimize this artifact. Continuing with metal artifacts in the field produces clinically unreliable results.
source: Standard pre-scan protocols; manufacturer artifact checklists

front: Bisphosphonates prevent bone loss by:
back:
Reducing the activity of osteoclasts

Bisphosphonates are antiresorptive agents that bind to hydroxyapatite in bone mineral and inhibit osteoclast function. Nitrogen-containing bisphosphonates (alendronate, risedronate, ibandronate, zoledronic acid) specifically inhibit osteoclast farnesyl pyrophosphate synthase. This reduces bone resorption, allowing bone formation to catch up and modestly increase BMD over time.
source: FDA bisphosphonate labels; ASBMR mechanism references

front: If a patient has a history of hyperparathyroidism, which of the following would be the preferred site to evaluate?
back:
33% (one-third) radius

The 33% radius (one-third radius) is the preferred site for evaluating primary hyperparathyroidism because it is approximately 95% cortical bone. PHPT preferentially depletes cortical bone via continuous PTH action, making the 33% radius the most sensitive site for detecting and monitoring this disease-specific cortical loss. ISCD 2023 specifically recommends including this site in PHPT evaluation.
source: ISCD 2023 forearm positions; Bilezikian PHPT Fifth Workshop

front: What is important for serial monitoring?
back:
Precision (reproducibility) of the measurement

Precision (reproducibility) is the critical metric for serial monitoring. The LSC (Least Significant Change = 2.77 x precision error) defines whether a measured BMD difference between scans represents a true biological change. Accuracy (trueness to actual value) matters more for diagnosis at a single time point but is less important for monitoring change over time on the same scanner.

front: What pharmaceutical may be suggested for a woman with high risk of breast cancer and elevated lipids?
back:
Evista (raloxifene, a SERM with breast benefit)

Raloxifene (Evista) is a Selective Estrogen Receptor Modulator (SERM) with three benefits: prevents bone loss (FDA approved for postmenopausal osteoporosis), reduces breast cancer risk (about 50% reduction in invasive breast cancer in high-risk women), and modestly improves lipid profile (lowers LDL). It is the only osteoporosis drug with explicit FDA breast cancer risk reduction labeling, making it ideal for this patient profile.
source: Evista FDA label; standard osteoporosis pharmacotherapy

front: What year was the Bone Mass Measurement Act proposed?
back:
1997 (BMMA established Medicare coverage)

The Bone Mass Measurement Act of 1997 established uniform Medicare Part B coverage for bone density measurement. It defined five qualifying clinical indications (estrogen deficiency, vertebral abnormalities, glucocorticoid therapy, hyperparathyroidism, drug monitoring) and the 23-month follow-up interval. The BMMA standardized DXA reimbursement across the US starting in 1998.
source: Medicare BMMA legislation; CMS coverage policy

front: Which of the following best describes low bone mass due to menopause?
back:
Primary osteoporosis Type I (postmenopausal, estrogen-related)

Primary osteoporosis Type I (postmenopausal osteoporosis) is caused by estrogen deficiency in postmenopausal women, preferentially affecting trabecular bone. Type II (senile osteoporosis) occurs after age 70 in both sexes, affecting both cortical and trabecular bone. Secondary osteoporosis is from underlying conditions (PHPT, hyperthyroidism, glucocorticoids, etc.).

front: Which of the following classes of cells are involved in bone remodeling?
back:
Osteoblasts, osteoclasts, and osteocytes together

Bone remodeling involves three cell types working in coordination within the Basic Multicellular Unit (BMU): osteoclasts initiate the cycle by resorbing bone (about 1-3 weeks), osteoblasts then form new bone matrix and mineralize it (about 3-4 months), and osteocytes embedded in the matrix sense mechanical strain and signal further remodeling. The complete cycle takes 3-6 months.
source: Standard bone biology; BMU cycle physiology

front: Which of the following therapies mimics the effects of estrogen on bone and has antiestrogenic effects in other tissue?
back:
Raloxifene (Selective Estrogen Receptor Modulator)

Raloxifene is a Selective Estrogen Receptor Modulator (SERM) that activates estrogen receptors in bone (preserves BMD, antiresorptive) while blocking them in breast tissue (reduces breast cancer risk by approximately 50% in high-risk women). It also has neutral or favorable effects on lipids. Raloxifene's tissue-selective activity makes it uniquely useful for postmenopausal women who also want breast cancer risk reduction.
source: Evista FDA label; ASBMR drug references

front: In both men and women, bone mass increases until approximately what age?
back:
30 years (peak bone mass achieved)

Peak bone mass is achieved at approximately age 30 in both sexes, after which gradual bone loss begins. Roughly 90% of peak bone mass is achieved by age 18, with the final 10% acquired between ages 18-30. After peak, men lose bone gradually (about 0.5% per year); women lose bone rapidly in the first 5-10 years postmenopause (about 2-3% per year), then more gradually.

front: If you are fortunate to get a new DXA unit, what must be performed in order to compare studies?
back:
Cross-calibration between the old and new scanners

Cross-calibration establishes a quantitative relationship between the old and new scanners so that prior patient baselines remain valid for longitudinal comparison. Same-manufacturer same-model replacement requires 10 phantom scans on each. Different-manufacturer or different-model replacement requires the more extensive 30-patient in vivo study (once on old, twice on new) within 60 days.

front: Which acronym describes the least amount of BMD change that can be statistically significant?
back:
LSC (Least Significant Change)

Least Significant Change (LSC) is the minimum BMD change above measurement uncertainty at 95% confidence. LSC = 2.77 x precision error (RMS-SD). Any change less than the LSC is within measurement noise and not interpretable as true biological change. CV expresses precision; SD describes dispersion; PE is the input to the LSC formula but is not itself the threshold for significance.

front: What are the two types of bone?
back:
Cortical and trabecular (the two distinct bone types)

The two types of bone are cortical (compact, dense outer shell, 80% of skeletal mass) and trabecular (cancellous, spongy internal network, 20% of skeletal mass). The other options pair synonyms or list cell types (not bone types). Cortical bone has slow turnover; trabecular bone has rapid turnover and is the primary site of early osteoporotic loss.

front: When a patient comes back for a follow-up study, what parameter is used to calculate the rate of change?
back:
BMD (the actual measured value for rate of change)

BMD is the parameter used for calculating rate of change in follow-up scans. The change is computed as (Follow-up BMD minus Baseline BMD), then compared to the LSC to determine statistical significance. T-scores and Z-scores are derived from BMD against reference populations and are not used directly for monitoring; they can shift when reference databases update independent of true biological change.

front: The shoulders of a patient should be squared with their:
back:
Pelvis (for symmetric AP positioning)

The shoulders should be squared with the pelvis to ensure symmetric anteroposterior positioning of the spine and consistent ROI placement. Rotation of the torso (shoulders not aligned with pelvis) creates an oblique projection that distorts vertebral identification and BMD calculation. Proper alignment of shoulders, hips, and feet is part of standard spine positioning.

front: What are the preferred sites for the measurement of bone density in children?
back:
Spine and total body less head (TBLH)

ISCD 2019 Pediatric Positions recommend lumbar spine and total body less head (TBLH) as the preferred sites in children. The proximal femur is generally avoided in pediatrics because skeletal immaturity and rapid growth complicate hip ROI placement. The forearm can be used in special circumstances but is not a routine pediatric site.

front: What percentage of the skeletal body mass is cortical bone?
back:
80 percent of total skeletal mass

Cortical bone comprises approximately 80% of total skeletal mass; trabecular bone makes up the remaining 20%. Despite cortical bone's larger mass fraction, trabecular bone has higher surface area and metabolic activity (about 8 times the turnover rate), making trabecular sites (spine, ultradistal radius) more responsive to therapy and more sensitive to early bone loss.

front: Which of the following is considered an uncontrollable risk factor for osteoporosis?
back:
Race and ethnicity

Race and ethnicity are unmodifiable risk factors for osteoporosis. Caucasian and Asian women have the highest risk; African American women generally have higher peak bone mass and lower age-adjusted fracture rates. Other unmodifiable factors include age, sex (female), family history, prior fragility fracture, and certain genetic conditions. Exercise, smoking, alcohol, and diet are all modifiable.

topic: BMD reported g per cm squared
front: On a DXA report, the bone mineral density is expressed as:
back:
g/cm squared (areal BMD units)

Areal BMD is expressed in g/cm squared (grams per centimeter squared). It is calculated as BMC/Area. Per ISCD 2023, BMD is reported to 3 decimal places (e.g., 0.987 g/cm-squared). BMC alone is in grams; Area alone is in cm-squared. True volumetric density (g/cm-cubed) is provided by QCT, not DXA.

front: How should you perform an in vivo precision study?
back:
Scan 30 patients twice each, OR 15 patients three times each

ISCD requires either 30 patients scanned twice (with repositioning between scans) OR 15 patients scanned three times (also with repositioning). Both protocols yield 30 degrees of freedom for the RMS-SD calculation. Scanning patients once each does not measure precision (no repeat). The 25-day phantom protocol establishes the QC baseline, not in vivo precision.

front: When analyzing the PA spine, which of the following is a no can do?
back:
Width is operator's choice (should be auto/standardized)

ROI width is NOT operator's choice in modern analysis. The width must be set by standardized algorithm parameters to ensure consistent area measurement across patients and follow-up scans. Other items listed are acceptable: spine should be straight and centered, the top line is placed between T12 and L1, and the bottom line between L4 and L5. Operator-driven width selection would invalidate precision.
source: ISCD 2023 spine analysis; manufacturer ROI standards

front: How many places to the right of the decimal point is used in reporting BMD?
back:
3 (the ISCD standard for BMD reporting)

Per ISCD 2023, BMD is reported to 3 decimal places (e.g., 0.987 g/cm-squared). The full reporting standard: BMD 3 decimals, BMC 2 decimals, Area 2 decimals, T-score 1 decimal, Z-score 1 decimal, percent reference values as integers. These standardize reporting and prevent over- or under-precision in clinical communication.

topic: Forearm sites based on ulna length
front: The names of the forearm sites are based on what?
back:
The location of the site on the bone in relation to the overall length of the ulna

Forearm site names are based on location along the ulna length, measured from the ulnar styloid (wrist) to the olecranon (elbow). The 33% radius is at one-third of this ulnar length from the styloid. The naming convention uses the ulna because it provides a longer, straighter reference axis than the curved radius. The actual measurement is then taken at the corresponding location on the radius.
source: ISCD 2023 forearm positions; manufacturer forearm protocols

front: A postmenopausal woman with a femoral neck T-score of -2.0 SD below young adult is classified as:
back:
Osteopenia (low bone mass)

A T-score of -2.0 falls in the WHO osteopenia (low bone mass) range, defined as T-score between -1.0 and -2.5. Osteoporosis requires T-score of -2.5 or lower. Severe osteoporosis additionally requires a fragility fracture. The osteopenia classification does not automatically indicate treatment; FRAX or clinical risk factor assessment guides management decisions for osteopenic patients.

front: What happens when there is an imbalance or uncoupling of the remodeling cycle?
back:
The usual result is a net loss in bone mass

Uncoupling of the BMU remodeling cycle (where resorption exceeds formation, or formation lags resorption) typically results in net bone loss. In healthy adults, resorption and formation are balanced. In osteoporosis, the balance shifts toward resorption: estrogen deficiency, glucocorticoids, hyperparathyroidism, and aging all uncouple the cycle in favor of net loss. Anabolic agents (teriparatide) attempt to reverse this by stimulating formation.

front: A technologist should perform a precision study in order to:
back:
Calculate the LSC for valid clinical monitoring

The primary purpose of a precision study is to measure the technologist's precision error and calculate the facility-specific Least Significant Change (LSC = 2.77 x precision error). The LSC determines whether measured BMD changes between scans represent true biological change or measurement noise. Shifts and drifts are detected by daily phantom QA, not by in vivo precision studies.

front: A DXA scanner that uses a single detector and a pinhole collimator is a:
back:
Pencil beam scanner with single detector

Pencil-beam scanners use a single detector with a pinhole collimator and acquire data point-by-point in a rectilinear pattern (slower but geometrically undistorted). Fan-beam scanners use a wedge-shaped beam with a multi-element detector array (faster, but introduces slight magnification distortion off the central ray). Pencil-beam is older technology; most modern DXA systems use fan-beam.
source: Standard DXA physics; manufacturer scanner specifications

front: Regarding the Shewhart Rules, the control limits are based on what percentage of the average values?
back:
1.5 percent of mean (standard Shewhart tolerance for DXA QC)

Shewhart control charts for DXA QC typically use 1.5% of the established baseline mean as the control limit (Lewiecki 2016). A single phantom value falling outside the +/- 1.5% band triggers investigation. Other Shewhart rules detect patterns of drift or shift even when individual values remain within the 1.5% band: 10 consecutive points on one side of mean (shift) or 5 consecutive points trending in one direction (trend).
source: Lewiecki 2016 J Clin Densitom; Shewhart QC standards

front: Which of the following terms best describes the sum of the data values divided by the number of values?
back:
Mean (arithmetic average)

The mean (arithmetic average) is calculated by summing all values and dividing by the number of values. Standard deviation measures dispersion around the mean (square root of variance). Variance is the average of squared deviations. Coefficient of variation = SD/Mean (expressed as percentage). All four are fundamental descriptive statistics in DXA QC analysis.

front: A DXA system that employs a rare earth filtered X-ray source uses what type of detector?
back:
Current integrating detector

GE Lunar k-edge filtered systems use current-integrating detectors that measure the cumulative current produced by all photons during the integration interval. Hologic energy-switching systems use pulse-counting detectors that count individual photons at each kVp. Both detector types and both energy-generation methods (k-edge and energy switching) produce valid DXA measurements via dual-energy subtraction.

front: The characteristic shapes of the vertebrae seen on the DXA image are primarily based on the appearance of the:
back:
Posterior elements (spinous process and laminae)

The characteristic vertebral shapes (L1 narrow, L2 broader, L3 with rectangular silhouette, L4 with X or H pattern) are primarily based on posterior elements (spinous processes, laminae, transverse processes) that project onto the PA DXA image. The vertebral bodies themselves are relatively uniform; the distinctive shapes come from posterior elements overlying them. This helps with vertebral labeling.
source: Standard DXA image interpretation; manufacturer reference

front: All of the following are true about accuracy in DXA EXCEPT:
back:
Accuracy is affected by the technologist's positioning skill

The EXCEPTION: technologist positioning primarily affects PRECISION (reproducibility), not accuracy (trueness to value). Accuracy depends on the underlying scanner technology and calibration (the reference standard used by the manufacturer). The other statements are correct: accuracy matters more for diagnosis (single-time-point measurement), precision matters more for serial monitoring (LSC comparison).

front: In both men and women, bone mass increases until approximately what age?
back:
30 years (peak bone mass achieved)

Peak bone mass is achieved at approximately age 30 in both sexes. About 90% of peak is achieved by age 18; the final 10% is acquired during the third decade of life. After peak, men lose bone gradually; women lose bone rapidly in the first 5-10 years postmenopause due to estrogen withdrawal. Achieving optimal peak bone mass is a primary prevention strategy against later osteoporosis.

front: Cortical bone makes up what?
back:
Outer dense cover of bones

Cortical bone forms the dense outer shell (cover) of all bones, including the shafts of long bones. The inner spongy core, especially in vertebrae and the ends of long bones, is trabecular (cancellous) bone. Cortical bone provides structural strength and protection; trabecular bone provides mechanical support with high surface area for metabolic exchange.

front: What percentage of trabecular bone is found in the PA spine as measured by DXA?
back:
66 percent of vertebral body composition

The PA lumbar spine measured by DXA includes vertebral bodies (approximately 66% trabecular) and posterior elements (mostly cortical), giving an overall composition of roughly 50-66% trabecular bone. This high trabecular fraction makes the spine the most metabolically active and most responsive site to therapy, which is why it is preferred for monitoring treatment effects.

front: The forearm, pelvis, and scapula all belong to which skeletal classification?
back:
Appendicular skeleton (limbs and supporting girdles)

The appendicular skeleton includes the upper and lower limbs (including the forearm) plus the pectoral girdle (scapula, clavicle) and pelvic girdle (pelvis). The axial skeleton includes the skull, vertebral column, ribs, and sternum. 'Central' and 'weight-bearing' are not standard anatomical classifications. DXA measures both: spine (axial), hip/forearm (appendicular).

front: The World Health Organization criteria for diagnosing osteoporosis was initially developed to apply to:
back:
Only postmenopausal Caucasian women

The WHO 1994 T-score criteria were originally developed and validated in postmenopausal Caucasian women. They have since been extended by ISCD to men aged 50+ and to women of other ethnicities, though some debate continues about ethnic database validity. The original derivation cohort and the dominant fracture epidemiology data informed the -2.5 SD threshold for postmenopausal Caucasian women.

front: In order to do a compare analysis:
back:
All of the above are required

Valid compare analysis requires all three: a previous scan of the same anatomical site, retrieval of the baseline study for direct comparison, and consistent patient positioning matching the baseline. The copy/compare function on most scanners automates retrieval and helps match positioning. Any missing element invalidates the LSC-based comparison. Compare analysis is essential for monitoring treatment response.
source: ISCD 2023 monitoring positions; manufacturer compare functions

front: How many degrees should you rotate the femur when positioning for a proximal femur scan?
back:
15-25 degrees (standard internal rotation)

15-25 degrees of internal rotation is the ISCD-standard for proximal femur positioning. This brings the femoral neck parallel to the scan table and hides the lesser trochanter posteriorly, producing the longest neck projection. The hip positioner device helps reproduce this angle consistently across scans. Variation outside 15-25 degrees risks foreshortening or distortion.

front: When should Z-scores be used to make a diagnosis, instead of T-scores?
back:
Males or females under age 20 (Z-scores required)

Per ISCD 2019 Pediatric Positions, T-scores must NEVER be used in patients under age 20. Z-scores compared to age-, sex-, and ethnicity-matched pediatric reference data are required. The rationale: T-scores reference young adult peak bone mass, which is inappropriate for skeletons that haven't reached peak. Z-scores are also used in premenopausal women (any age under 50 for women) and men under 50.

front: What is the name of the isotope used in DPA (dual photon absorptiometry)?
back:
Gadolinium-153 (the DPA isotope)

Dual Photon Absorptiometry (DPA) used Gadolinium-153 (Gd-153) as the radioisotope source, providing two photon energies (44 keV and 100 keV) needed for dual-energy subtraction of soft tissue. DPA was the predecessor to DXA and was replaced because DXA's X-ray tube produces higher photon flux (faster scans) and avoids the cost and decay limitations of radioisotopes. Iodine-125 was used in single photon absorptiometry (SPA).

front: Which of the following bone parameters is measured in grams?
back:
BMC (Bone Mineral Content, in grams)

Bone Mineral Content (BMC) is the absolute mass of bone mineral in the scanned region, measured in grams. Area is measured in cm-squared. BMD (the derived ratio BMC/Area) is in g/cm-squared. Per ISCD 2023, BMC is reported to 2 decimal places. These three parameters together describe the bone measurement: BMC tells you how much mineral, Area tells you the projected footprint, BMD normalizes mineral by footprint.

front: The region of the proximal femur lying on the border of the femoral neck and the greater trochanter is termed:
back:
Ward's area (Ward's triangle in the femoral neck region)

Ward's area (or Ward's triangle) is a small region of highly trabecular bone in the femoral neck, near the junction with the greater trochanter. It is software-defined rather than anatomical. Ward's area is not used for diagnosis due to poor reproducibility. The femoral neck and total hip are the standard diagnostic ROIs in the proximal femur.
source: ISCD 2023 hip reporting; standard hip anatomy

front: Which of the following has the highest percentage of cortical bone?
back:
33% radius (about 95% cortical)

The 33% radius is approximately 95% cortical bone, the highest cortical fraction among standard DXA sites. This is why ISCD recommends the 33% radius for monitoring primary hyperparathyroidism, which preferentially depletes cortical bone. The femoral neck is mixed, fingers vary, and whole body is an average. The ultradistal radius is contrastingly approximately 50-66% trabecular.

front: In making a diagnosis for children, what are some guidelines?
back:
All of the above are pediatric guidelines

Per ISCD 2019 Pediatric Positions, all of these apply: (1) Z-scores are used exclusively (T-scores never used in patients under 20), (2) 'Low bone density for chronologic age' is preferred over osteopenia/osteoporosis labels that imply pathology, and (3) pediatric-specific software with age-, sex-, and ethnicity-matched reference databases is required. Adult databases are inappropriate.

topic: BMD equals BMC divided by area
front: Which of the following is correct?
back:
BMD equals BMC divided by Area

Areal BMD = BMC (in grams) divided by projected bone Area (in cm-squared), expressed as g/cm-squared. This is the fundamental DXA calculation. BMD is therefore lower when area is larger for the same BMC, which is why DXA systematically underestimates true volumetric density in small bones (children, short adults) and overestimates in large bones.

front: What is the age range used with FRAX?
back:
40-90 years (FRAX validated range)

FRAX is validated for ages 40-90 in untreated adults. Below age 40, fracture probability is too low and not well predicted by the clinical risk factors used in FRAX. Above age 90, data are sparse and competing mortality dominates. FRAX inputs do not include certain risk factors (e.g., falls history, vitamin D status, biochemistry), and ISCD recommends clinical judgment alongside FRAX output.

front: To determine the least significant change at 95% confidence level (for baseline and one follow-up scan), the precision error is multiplied by:
back:
2.77 (the LSC multiplier for 95% CI)

LSC = 2.77 x precision error. The 2.77 factor combines 1.96 (z-score for 95% confidence) multiplied by sqrt(2) (because two independent measurements are compared, baseline and follow-up: 1.96 x 1.414 = 2.77). This is the standard ISCD formula for two-time-point LSC at 95% confidence.

front: What skeletal site may be preferred for assessing early menopause following an oophorectomy?
back:
Spine (trabecular-rich, rapid response)

Surgical menopause from bilateral oophorectomy causes abrupt, severe estrogen withdrawal that preferentially affects trabecular bone. The lumbar spine, with about 66% trabecular content, shows the most rapid and measurable BMD loss in this scenario. Spine BMD is therefore the most sensitive site for baseline and follow-up after oophorectomy, similar to its preferred role in monitoring antiresorptive therapy.
source: ISCD 2023 monitoring positions; standard postmenopausal references

topic: Net bone gain formation more than resorption
front: An increase in the overall bone mass of a patient is closely related to an accelerated rate of bone:
back:
Formation and a reduced amount of resorption

Net bone gain requires bone formation to exceed bone resorption. Anabolic therapies (teriparatide, romosozumab) stimulate formation directly. Antiresorptive therapies (bisphosphonates, denosumab) reduce resorption, allowing formation to relatively dominate. Either approach shifts the balance toward net gain. Mechanical loading (Wolff's law) also stimulates formation. The other answer options use imprecise terminology.

front: Which of the following measures more trabecular bone?
back:
QCT spine (isolates trabecular bone in the vertebral body)

QCT (Quantitative Computed Tomography) can specifically measure trabecular-only bone in the vertebral body, excluding posterior cortical elements. This trabecular isolation is impossible with DXA, which projects the entire vertebra in 2D. PA DXA spine is approximately 50-66% trabecular (mixed). Lateral spine DXA has less posterior overlap but still includes cortical bone. VFA assesses morphology, not BMD.
source: Standard QCT physics; ISCD QCT positions

front: Which of the following conditions can contribute to the development of osteoporosis EXCEPT:
back:
Hypergonadism in males (elevated sex hormones protect bone)

Hypergonadism (excess sex hormones) protects bone and does NOT cause osteoporosis. HYPOGONADISM (LOW testosterone in men, LOW estrogen in women) causes bone loss. The other conditions all cause secondary osteoporosis: hyperparathyroidism depletes cortical bone, multiple myeloma causes lytic lesions, and rheumatoid arthritis combines inflammation-driven bone loss with frequent glucocorticoid therapy.

topic: DXA both fracture risk and monitoring
front: Which of the following statements is true regarding DXA?
back:
DXA can be used to predict fracture risk and to evaluate the effectiveness of treatment

DXA is used both for fracture risk prediction (T-score and FRAX) AND for monitoring treatment effectiveness (BMD change vs LSC). DXA uses an X-ray tube (not a radioactive source like SPA or DPA). DXA is both faster AND more precise than DPA. DXA can also predict fracture risk at the forearm and through total body composition; not just hip and spine.

topic: Exercise weight bearing and resistance restructured
front: Which combination of exercises is best for bone health?
back:
Weight-bearing impact (running, dancing) plus muscle-strengthening resistance training

Optimal bone health exercise combines weight-bearing impact activity (running, jumping, dancing, racket sports) with resistance training (weights, bands, or body weight). Impact loading stimulates bone formation via mechanical strain; resistance training strengthens muscle attachments and improves balance. Swimming and cycling, while excellent cardiovascular exercises, provide minimal bone-loading stimulus because of buoyancy and seated mechanics.
source: BHOF 2022 exercise recommendations; standard bone health references

front: What are the four classifications that apply to the heel?
back:
Appendicular, peripheral, predominantly trabecular and weight-bearing

The calcaneus (heel) is classified as: APPENDICULAR (lower limb is appendicular skeleton), PERIPHERAL (not a central DXA site), PREDOMINANTLY TRABECULAR (high trabecular content), and WEIGHT-BEARING (loads body weight). These four classifications together describe the heel's role in skeletal anatomy and physiology. QUS heel measurement is screening only per ISCD, not diagnostic.
source: Standard skeletal classification; ISCD QUS positions

front: Currently, the most accurate method to determine a patient's response to therapy for osteoporosis is:
back:
Serial DXA measurements of the proximal femur and spine

Serial DXA of the proximal femur and spine is the standard method for monitoring osteoporosis treatment response. The trabecular-rich lumbar spine shows the largest treatment-induced BMD changes and is the most sensitive monitoring site. Hip is added for completeness. Forearm is too cortical (slow turnover) to detect therapy response. QCT and bone turnover markers are adjuncts, not standard monitoring tools per ISCD.

front: DXA results may be influenced by:
back:
All of the above

All listed factors influence DXA results. Body thickness affects photon attenuation and scan mode selection. Degenerative changes (osteophytes, facet hypertrophy, vascular calcification) falsely elevate spine BMD. Compression fractures concentrate bone into smaller area, also falsely elevating BMD. Scan mode (Standard, Thick, Express) affects acquisition parameters and must be matched between baseline and follow-up.

front: Sunlight is the main source of vitamin:
back:
D (synthesized in skin from UVB exposure)

Vitamin D is synthesized in the skin when 7-dehydrocholesterol is converted to cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) by UVB radiation from sunlight. This endogenous production is the main natural source of vitamin D; dietary sources (fatty fish, fortified dairy, supplements) are secondary. Vitamin D deficiency contributes to osteomalacia and osteoporosis. BHOF recommends 800-1000 IU/day vitamin D supplementation for adults over 50.

front: All follow-up DXA scans should be compared to:
back:
The baseline scan as the reference

ISCD specifies follow-up scans should be compared to the BASELINE scan (the original reference study), not to intermediate or most-recent scans. Comparing to baseline allows evaluation of overall trend and treatment effect since the initial assessment. Comparing only to the most recent scan may miss longer-term patterns. Some reports include both comparisons, but the baseline comparison is the standard.

front: With long-term use of steroids, what type of bone would be affected?
back:
Trabecular bone preferentially in early phase

Long-term glucocorticoid therapy preferentially affects TRABECULAR bone in the early phase, particularly at the spine. This is why the lumbar spine is the most sensitive monitoring site for glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis. Cortical bone is also eventually affected, but trabecular loss occurs first and most rapidly. The mechanism involves reduced osteoblast function, increased osteoblast apoptosis, and impaired calcium absorption.
source: ACR GIO guidelines; standard glucocorticoid bone references

front: If a patient has a history of a laminectomy, how would that affect the results?
back:
Decrease BMD because posterior bone is removed

Laminectomy surgically removes the lamina and often the spinous process, reducing the bone in the spine ROI. Since DXA measures all bone in the ROI (including posterior elements), removing bone reduces measured BMC and falsely lowers BMD at the affected vertebra. The vertebra must be excluded from analysis; remaining valid vertebrae provide the diagnostic average.

front: Fall prevention should always be part of a patient's management with low bone mass, which should include talking to them about:
back:
All of the above

Comprehensive fall prevention addresses multiple environmental factors: grab bars (especially bathrooms and stairways), adequate lighting (night lights, motion-activated lights), and removing trip hazards (loose rugs, clutter, cords). Intrinsic factors (vision, balance, sedating medications, polypharmacy) also require attention. Fall prevention reduces fracture risk independent of BMD treatment and is a core patient education topic.

front: In the United States, approximately how many people are affected with low bone mass?
back:
54 million Americans affected

Approximately 54 million Americans have low bone mass (osteopenia or osteoporosis) per NHANES and BHOF estimates. Of these, about 10 million have osteoporosis (8 million women, 2 million men), and about 44 million have osteopenia. The numbers underscore osteoporosis as a major public health issue, with approximately 2 million fragility fractures per year in the US.

front: Alendronate is the only anabolic agent approved for treatment of osteoporosis in the United States.
back:
False

FALSE. Alendronate (Fosamax) is a bisphosphonate, an ANTIRESORPTIVE agent, NOT anabolic. Anabolic agents approved in the US are teriparatide (Forteo), abaloparatide (Tymlos), and romosozumab (Evenity, which is dual-action). Bisphosphonates inhibit bone resorption by osteoclasts; anabolic agents stimulate bone formation by osteoblasts.
source: FDA drug labels; ASBMR drug classification

topic: Single vertebra not for diagnosis - false
front: You may use one vertebral body to make a diagnosis.
back:
False

FALSE. Per ISCD 2023, a minimum of two evaluable contiguous vertebrae is required for a valid lumbar spine BMD diagnosis. A single vertebra is never used due to high inter-vertebral variability. If only one valid vertebra remains after exclusions, the spine cannot be used for diagnosis and other sites (hip, 33% radius) must be relied upon.

front: It is suggested that you archive once a year.
back:
False

FALSE. Archiving once a year is too infrequent. Best practice is regular (daily or weekly) backup of patient scans and QC data to prevent data loss from hardware failure. PACS integration provides automatic continuous archiving. The 2022 ARRT Image Production section explicitly addresses file and database management including storage, retrieval, and backup protocols.
source: ARRT 2022 BD Content Specifications; standard data management

front: The heel is the only validated skeletal site for the clinical use of QUS in osteoporosis management.
back:
True

TRUE. The calcaneus (heel) is the only ISCD-validated skeletal site for QUS in clinical osteoporosis management. Other QUS sites (radius, tibia, phalanges) have been studied but do not have the same evidence base or fracture-prediction validation. Note that QUS is screening only per ISCD; it is not diagnostic and does not replace central DXA for WHO-criteria diagnosis.

front: Z-scores (not T-scores) should always be used in pediatric DXA reports.
back:
True

TRUE. Per ISCD 2019 Pediatric Positions, Z-scores must be used in patients under 20 (some sources cite under 18 or under 19 for the cutoff). T-scores are never appropriate in pediatrics because they reference young-adult peak bone mass, which has not been achieved in children. Z-scores compare to age-, sex-, and ethnicity-matched pediatric reference data.

front: Measuring patient's height is not important in bone densitometry.
back:
False

FALSE. Measuring height is very important in bone densitometry. Historical height loss greater than 4 cm (1.5 inches) versus tallest adult height is a strong indicator of vertebral compression fractures and an ISCD indication for VFA. Acute height loss of 2 cm or more between visits also indicates VFA. Height is also a FRAX input (used to calculate BMI). Patient height should be measured accurately at every DXA visit.

front: The measure of the variability or the spread of data around the mean is termed standard deviation.
back:
True

TRUE. Standard deviation (SD) quantifies the spread or variability of data around the mean. It is the square root of the variance. In DXA, SD is used in T-scores (number of SDs from young-adult mean), Z-scores (number of SDs from age-matched mean), and precision error (RMS-SD for in vivo precision studies). 1 SD typically equals about 10-12% of the mean BMD value.

front: In regards to the International Society For Clinical Densitometry 2015 Official Positions, the WHO diagnostic criteria may be applied to women in the menopause transition.
back:
True

TRUE. Per ISCD positions, the WHO T-score criteria may be applied to women in the menopause transition (perimenopause), not just to fully postmenopausal women. This recognizes that bone loss begins in the years preceding final menopause and that risk assessment is clinically warranted in this transitional period. WHO criteria still require postmenopausal status by the time of formal diagnosis.

front: The weight limit for the DXA table is manufacturer specific.
back:
True

TRUE. DXA table weight limits vary by manufacturer and model: Hologic Horizon is 450 lb (204 kg), GE Lunar iDXA is 450 lb, GE Lunar Prodigy is 350 lb, GE Lunar DPX is 300 lb. Exceeding the weight limit risks mechanical damage and inaccurate scanning geometry. For patients above the limit, the forearm can be scanned off-table with the patient seated.

front: When analyzing the proximal femur, the box measuring the neck should include the greater trochanter.
back:
False

FALSE. The femoral neck ROI must NOT include the greater trochanter. Including the trochanter falsely alters BMC and projected area at the neck. The neck ROI is centered across the narrowest portion of the femoral neck, perpendicular to the neck axis, with both ends sitting cleanly within neck bone. The greater trochanter has its own ROI in the total hip analysis.
source: ISCD 2023 hip acquisition; manufacturer ROI guides

topic: Fan beam not point by point false
front: With fan beam scanners, scanning occurs point by point.
back:
False

FALSE. Fan-beam scanners use a wedge-shaped diverging X-ray beam and a multi-element detector array, acquiring a line of data simultaneously rather than point-by-point. This is much faster than pencil-beam scanning. PENCIL-BEAM scanners use a single detector with a pinhole collimator and acquire data point-by-point in a rectilinear pattern, which is slower but geometrically undistorted.
source: Standard DXA physics; fan-beam vs pencil-beam

front: Discordance is a term that is rarely used in bone densitometry.
back:
False

FALSE. Discordance is a commonly used term in bone densitometry. It refers to the situation where a patient's T-scores at different skeletal sites fall into different WHO diagnostic categories (e.g., osteoporosis at spine but only osteopenia at hip). Per ISCD, diagnosis follows the lowest qualifying T-score from the standard sites. Discordance is frequent and clinically important.

topic: Hip axis length increases fracture risk true
front: As the hip axis length increases, the risk of hip fractures increases independent of BMD.
back:
True

TRUE. Hip Axis Length (HAL) is the distance from the inner pelvic brim to the outer surface of the greater trochanter along the femoral neck axis. Longer HAL is independently associated with increased hip fracture risk in some populations, particularly Caucasian women, due to biomechanical leverage during a sideways fall. HAL is reported on some DXA reports but is not a standard ISCD diagnostic parameter.

front: The correct term for the scanning pattern with pencil beam DXA units is rectilinear.
back:
True

TRUE. Pencil-beam scanners acquire data point-by-point in a RECTILINEAR pattern: the X-ray source and detector move together in a serpentine path covering the scan field, with the beam traveling in a straight (rectilinear) raster pattern. This is slower than fan-beam acquisition but produces geometrically undistorted images.
source: Standard DXA physics; pencil-beam scanner specifications

front: T-scores should be used in serial monitoring.
back:
False

FALSE. T-scores are used for diagnosis at a single time point but should NOT be used for serial monitoring. Monitoring should use absolute BMD values compared to the LSC. T-scores can shift when reference databases update or are recalibrated, independent of true biological change in the patient. Comparing BMD directly avoids this confounder.

front: Sievert refers to effective dose.
back:
True

TRUE. The Sievert (Sv) is the SI unit for both dose equivalent and effective dose. Effective dose accounts for the biological effect of radiation by multiplying absorbed dose (Gray) by a radiation weighting factor (Wr) AND further weighting for tissue radiosensitivity (Wt). For X-rays, Wr = 1, so 1 Gy = 1 Sv. Central DXA effective dose is typically 1-10 microSieverts (uSv), an extremely low value.
source: ICRP radiation units; standard radiation physics

front: The ultra distal part of the forearm is mostly cortical bone.
back:
False

FALSE. The ultradistal radius is approximately 50-66 percent TRABECULAR bone, not cortical. The high trabecular content makes it metabolically active. In contrast, the 33% radius (proximal forearm) is approximately 95% CORTICAL bone and is the ISCD-designated diagnostic site, particularly sensitive for primary hyperparathyroidism which depletes cortical bone.

front: Which type of bone is lost primarily in Type 1 primary osteoporosis?
back:
Trabecular bone of vertebrae and metaphyses

Type 1 (postmenopausal) primary osteoporosis preferentially affects trabecular bone due to increased osteoclast activity from estrogen deficiency. Trabecular bone has a high surface-to-volume ratio, making it more vulnerable to rapid turnover-driven loss. Type 2 (senile) osteoporosis affects both cortical and trabecular bone in patients over 70.
source: Standard osteoporosis classification; Riggs and Melton

front: Type 2 primary osteoporosis (senile osteoporosis) typically occurs in patients aged:
back:
70 and older in both sexes

Type 2 (senile) osteoporosis occurs in both sexes aged 70 and older. Mechanisms include age-related decline in osteoblast function, reduced calcium absorption from declining vitamin D activation, secondary hyperparathyroidism, and gradual loss of mechanical loading from reduced activity. Unlike Type 1, Type 2 affects both cortical and trabecular bone.

front: Osteocytes are bone cells that primarily function to:
back:
Sense mechanical strain and signal remodeling

Osteocytes are the most abundant bone cells (90 percent), embedded within the mineralized matrix. They function primarily as mechanosensors detecting mechanical strain and fluid flow through the lacunocanalicular network, then signaling osteoblasts and osteoclasts to remodel bone in response. Osteocytes also regulate phosphate homeostasis via FGF23 secretion. Osteoblasts form bone; osteoclasts resorb it.
source: Standard bone biology; osteocyte mechanobiology references

front: Bone modeling differs from bone remodeling in that modeling:
back:
Shapes bone during growth without resorption-formation coupling

Bone modeling occurs primarily during growth and shapes bone via independent osteoblast formation and osteoclast resorption at different surfaces, without the resorption-then-formation coupling of remodeling. Modeling dominates childhood and adolescence; remodeling is the lifelong process that replaces about 10 percent of the skeleton per year in adults. Both processes contribute to skeletal health.

front: Which signaling molecule directly stimulates osteoclast formation and activity?
back:
RANKL (receptor activator of NF-kB ligand)

RANKL, produced primarily by osteoblasts and osteocytes, binds RANK receptors on osteoclast precursors to drive their differentiation and activation. OPG acts as a decoy receptor that binds RANKL and prevents osteoclast activation, balancing the system. Denosumab (Prolia) is a monoclonal antibody against RANKL. Estrogen reduces RANKL and increases OPG, suppressing bone resorption.
source: Standard bone biology; ASBMR signaling references

front: Estrogen contributes to bone health primarily by:
back:
Suppressing RANKL and increasing OPG

Estrogen's primary skeletal action is suppression of RANKL (reducing osteoclast formation) and increased OPG (which binds and neutralizes any remaining RANKL). This shifts the RANKL/OPG balance toward bone preservation. Postmenopausal estrogen withdrawal removes this brake on resorption, leading to accelerated bone loss in the early postmenopausal years.
source: Standard endocrinology of bone; estrogen signaling references

front: Under the Bone Mass Measurement Act, which of the following is NOT one of the five qualifying conditions for Medicare-covered DXA?
back:
Family history of hip fracture

Family history alone is NOT a BMMA qualifying condition. The five BMMA indications are: (1) estrogen-deficient women at clinical risk, (2) vertebral abnormalities on imaging, (3) prolonged glucocorticoid therapy (5+ mg/day for 3+ months), (4) primary hyperparathyroidism, and (5) monitoring osteoporosis drug therapy. Family history is a FRAX input and clinical risk factor but does not independently qualify for Medicare coverage.
source: Medicare BMMA regulations; CMS coverage policy

front: Is pregnancy a contraindication to DXA scanning?
back:
No, DXA dose is too low to harm a fetus but is generally deferred

DXA effective dose (1-10 uSv) is far below thresholds for fetal harm, but standard practice is to defer DXA until after pregnancy unless clinically essential, following ALARA principles. Women of childbearing potential should be screened for pregnancy before scanning. If DXA is essential during pregnancy, the procedure can be performed with appropriate shielding and informed consent.
source: ICRP recommendations; standard pregnancy radiation references

front: Before initiating a DXA scan, the technologist should:
back:
Explain the procedure and obtain patient cooperation

Patient care begins with explaining the procedure, expected duration, need to remain still, and any positioning that will be required. Patient understanding ensures cooperation and reduces motion artifacts. Formal research consent is not required for clinical DXA. The technologist initiates and performs the scan, not the radiologist. Brief, clear communication produces better scans.
source: Standard patient care protocols; ISCD patient communication

front: Before scanning, the technologist must verify patient identity using:
back:
At least two patient identifiers per Joint Commission standards

Joint Commission and standard patient safety protocols require at least two patient identifiers before any procedure: typically name plus date of birth, name plus medical record number, or similar combinations. Room number, appearance alone, or single verbal confirmation are insufficient. This verification prevents wrong-patient scans, which would corrupt longitudinal data and risk patient harm.

front: Zoledronic acid (Reclast) is administered as:
back:
Annual intravenous infusion over at least 15 minutes

Zoledronic acid is given as a 5 mg intravenous infusion once yearly over at least 15 minutes. The slow infusion reduces risk of acute-phase reaction (flu-like symptoms within 24-72 hours). Patients must have adequate hydration and CrCl above 35 mL/min (contraindicated below). Annual dosing improves adherence versus daily/weekly oral bisphosphonates. Acetaminophen post-infusion helps manage acute-phase reactions.
source: Reclast FDA label; ASBMR bisphosphonate references

front: Proper administration of oral bisphosphonates (e.g., alendronate) requires:
back:
Taking on empty stomach with plain water, remaining upright 30 minutes

Oral bisphosphonates have very low absorption (less than 1 percent) and must be taken on an empty stomach (overnight fast) with 6-8 ounces of plain water (not coffee, juice, mineral water, or other beverages). The patient must remain upright (sit or stand) for at least 30 minutes after dosing to prevent esophageal irritation. Food and other beverages bind bisphosphonates and prevent absorption.

front: Denosumab (Prolia) for osteoporosis is administered as:
back:
Subcutaneous injection every 6 months

Denosumab is given as a 60 mg subcutaneous injection every 6 months. Unlike bisphosphonates, denosumab has no skeletal retention and its effects rapidly reverse on discontinuation, causing rebound bone loss and increased vertebral fracture risk. ISCD and ASBMR recommend that denosumab not be stopped without transitioning to another antiresorptive (typically a bisphosphonate) to prevent rebound fractures.
source: Prolia FDA label; ASBMR denosumab discontinuation position

front: Teriparatide (Forteo) is administered as:
back:
Daily subcutaneous self-injection

Teriparatide is a daily subcutaneous self-injection of 20 mcg. Intermittent daily PTH(1-34) is anabolic (stimulates osteoblasts), in contrast to continuous PTH excess (as in primary hyperparathyroidism) which is catabolic. The FDA removed the 24-month treatment cap and osteosarcoma boxed warning in November 2020. After anabolic therapy, sequential antiresorptive treatment is recommended to maintain gains.
source: Forteo FDA label (2020 update); ASBMR sequential therapy

front: Romosozumab (Evenity) is unique among osteoporosis medications because it:
back:
Both increases bone formation and decreases bone resorption

Romosozumab is a humanized monoclonal antibody against sclerostin that has DUAL action: it stimulates bone formation (anabolic) AND decreases bone resorption (antiresorptive). Dosed as 210 mg subcutaneously monthly for 12 months. Carries a boxed warning for cardiovascular events (myocardial infarction, stroke); should not be initiated within 1 year of MI or stroke. Sequential antiresorptive therapy is recommended after the 12-month course.
source: Evenity FDA label; ARCH and FRAME trial data

front: The BHOF-recommended daily calcium intake for postmenopausal women is approximately:
back:
1200 mg per day total intake from diet and supplements

BHOF and most professional societies recommend 1200 mg per day total calcium intake for postmenopausal women, ideally from dietary sources (dairy, leafy greens, fortified foods). Supplemental calcium fills gaps but excess can cause kidney stones and cardiovascular concerns. The tolerable upper intake level is 2000 mg per day. Calcium intake supports but does not replace osteoporosis medications.
source: BHOF 2022 Clinician's Guide; Institute of Medicine DRIs

front: The BHOF-recommended daily vitamin D intake for adults over 50 is approximately:
back:
800 to 1000 IU per day total intake

BHOF recommends 800-1000 IU per day of vitamin D3 for adults over 50, with target serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D level of at least 30 ng/mL. Higher doses may be needed for deficient patients. Vitamin D supports calcium absorption and direct effects on bone and muscle function. Severe deficiency causes osteomalacia; mild deficiency contributes to osteoporosis and fall risk.
source: BHOF 2022 Clinician's Guide; Endocrine Society vitamin D guidelines

front: Which class of medications is associated with secondary osteoporosis risk through impaired calcium absorption?
back:
Proton pump inhibitors (PPIs) used long-term

Long-term PPI use (omeprazole, esomeprazole, pantoprazole) reduces gastric acid, impairing the ionization of dietary calcium needed for absorption. Studies suggest a modest increase in hip and vertebral fracture risk with long-term PPI use. Other osteoporosis-associated medications include glucocorticoids, aromatase inhibitors, androgen deprivation therapy, antiepileptics, heparin, and certain immunosuppressants.
source: BHOF secondary causes; FDA PPI fracture safety communication

front: Androgen Deprivation Therapy (ADT) for prostate cancer is associated with:
back:
Rapid bone loss similar to surgical menopause in women

ADT (GnRH agonists like leuprolide, GnRH antagonists like degarelix, or orchiectomy) produces rapid bone loss analogous to surgical menopause in women. Testosterone suppression removes the protective effect on bone, leading to 2-5 percent BMD loss per year, particularly in the first year. Baseline DXA, periodic monitoring, and consideration of bone-protective therapy are standard in men starting ADT.
source: AUA/SUFU guidelines; standard ADT bone management

front: The three cardinal principles of radiation safety (ALARA triad) are:
back:
Time, distance, and shielding

ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable) is implemented through three principles: minimize TIME of exposure, maximize DISTANCE from the source, and use SHIELDING when appropriate. For DXA, time is fixed by scan parameters; distance is the primary operator protection (workstation behind a barrier or at maximum practical distance from the gantry); shielding is rarely needed due to the extremely low dose.
source: ICRP radiation protection; ISCD radiation safety white paper

front: The NRC annual occupational whole-body dose limit for radiation workers is:
back:
50 mSv per year (5 rem)

The NRC annual occupational whole-body effective dose limit is 50 mSv (5 rem) per year. Extremity dose limit is 500 mSv (50 rem) per year. ALARA principles require keeping actual exposures far below these limits. DXA operators typically receive less than 1 mSv per year due to the very low scatter from DXA scans and proper distance/shielding practices.

front: The average annual background radiation dose in the United States is approximately:
back:
3 mSv per year

Average annual background radiation in the US is approximately 3 mSv (3000 uSv) per year, primarily from radon (about 2/3 of background), terrestrial sources, cosmic rays, and internal radioactivity (K-40). A single DXA scan (1-10 uSv) is less than one day of background radiation. Comparing DXA to background helps patients contextualize the extremely low dose.

front: For a standard central DXA scan, lead apron shielding for the patient is:
back:
Not typically required due to the extremely low dose

Lead apron shielding is NOT typically required for central DXA because the dose is so low (1-10 uSv) and the scan field is narrowly collimated. Some facilities provide gonadal shielding as reassurance for patients of childbearing age, but it is not a clinical requirement. Pediatric scanning follows ALARA principles but does not mandate aprons. Patient education and reassurance about the low dose are more important.

front: If a technologist doubles their distance from the X-ray source, their exposure is reduced to:
back:
One-fourth (1/4) of original

The inverse square law states that radiation intensity decreases with the square of the distance. Doubling the distance reduces exposure to 1/4 (1 over 2-squared) of the original; tripling the distance reduces it to 1/9 (1 over 3-squared). This is why distance is such an effective protection strategy in DXA: even modest increases in operator-to-gantry distance produce large reductions in occupational dose.

front: Which is an ISCD indication for VFA in a postmenopausal woman?
back:
Historical height loss greater than 4 cm

ISCD indications for VFA include T-score below -1.0 plus height loss greater than 4 cm historical or 2 cm prospective, prior non-spine fragility fracture, or glucocorticoid therapy of 5+ mg/day for 3+ months. Women aged 70+ or men aged 80+ with T-score below -1.0 qualify, as does clinical suspicion of vertebral fracture. Routine VFA without indications is not recommended.

front: Compared to standard radiography, VFA has the advantage of:
back:
Significantly lower radiation dose

VFA delivers approximately 1/100 the radiation dose of standard lateral spine radiography (about 3-50 uSv for VFA vs 600 uSv for plain film). VFA also integrates with the DXA visit, requiring no separate appointment. The trade-off is lower spatial resolution: VFA is excellent for morphological grading (Genant) of fracture height but cannot detect subtle bone lesions; radiography is needed for definitive imaging of any suspicious finding.
source: ISCD 2023 VFA positions; comparative radiation dose data

front: The distribution of osteoporotic vertebral fractures along the spine is:
back:
Bimodal at T7-T8 and T12-L1

Osteoporotic vertebral fractures show a bimodal distribution along the spine, with peaks at the mid-thoracic (T7-T8) and thoracolumbar junction (T12-L1). These zones experience maximum biomechanical stress: mid-thoracic kyphosis and thoracolumbar transition between rigid thoracic and mobile lumbar segments. The T7-L4 range covered by VFA captures both peaks.

front: Vertebra plana refers to:
back:
Complete or near-complete vertebral body collapse (greater than 90 percent height loss)

Vertebra plana is the most severe form of vertebral compression fracture, with complete or near-complete collapse (greater than 90 percent height loss). It falls within Genant Grade 3 (severe, greater than 40 percent loss). Vertebra plana causes significant clinical impact including severe pain, kyphosis, restrictive lung disease, and substantial functional impairment. It strongly predicts subsequent fractures.

front: The three morphological types of vertebral compression fracture are:
back:
Wedge, biconcave, and crush

Vertebral compression fractures are classified by morphology as WEDGE (anterior height reduced, posterior preserved), BICONCAVE (middle height reduced, anterior and posterior preserved), and CRUSH (all three heights reduced). Wedge fractures are most common, often occurring at the thoracolumbar junction. Genant grading is applied to whichever measurement (anterior, middle, or posterior) shows the greatest height loss.

front: Anorexia nervosa causes bone loss primarily through:
back:
Hypothalamic amenorrhea with low estrogen, low IGF-1, and elevated cortisol

Anorexia causes complex hormonal disruption including hypothalamic amenorrhea (low estrogen from suppressed GnRH), low IGF-1 from undernutrition, elevated cortisol from stress and starvation response, and direct effects of low body weight on mechanical loading. The combination produces severe trabecular bone loss, often more pronounced than typical postmenopausal osteoporosis. Adolescents with anorexia may fail to achieve peak bone mass.
source: Standard eating disorder medicine; Endocrine Society guidelines

front: The female athlete triad consists of:
back:
Eating disorder, amenorrhea, and osteoporosis

The female athlete triad (now sometimes called RED-S, Relative Energy Deficiency in Sport) includes low energy availability with or without disordered eating, menstrual dysfunction including amenorrhea, and low BMD or osteoporosis. The underlying mechanism is hypothalamic amenorrhea from inadequate caloric intake relative to expenditure. Young athletes can develop osteoporosis despite high physical activity.
source: ACSM and FIMS position statements; IOC RED-S consensus

front: Bone density management in transgender patients on gender-affirming hormone therapy:
back:
Should consider the affirmed gender and current hormone therapy

Bone density management in transgender patients should consider the current hormone regimen (estrogen, testosterone, or absence) and duration of therapy. ISCD recommends using a Z-score with reference data appropriate to the affirmed gender after sufficient time on hormone therapy. Pre-hormone or recently-started patients may be more appropriately compared to natal-sex references. Clinical judgment guides interpretation in this evolving area.

front: Patients with spinal cord injury below the level of the lesion experience:
back:
Rapid bone loss especially in the first 1-2 years

Spinal cord injury causes rapid disuse osteoporosis below the lesion level, with the most rapid loss in the first 1-2 years (up to 40 percent in some sites). The mechanism is loss of mechanical loading via Wolff's law combined with autonomic dysregulation of bone metabolism. Fracture risk is markedly elevated, particularly at the distal femur and proximal tibia in paraplegics.
source: Standard SCI rehabilitation references; disuse osteoporosis

front: Chronic Kidney Disease - Mineral and Bone Disorder (CKD-MBD) involves all of the following EXCEPT:
back:
Increased bone formation rate

CKD-MBD typically causes complex bone disease with VARIABLE turnover (high turnover from secondary hyperparathyroidism in many patients; low turnover or adynamic bone disease in others). Mechanisms include phosphate retention (driving FGF23 elevation), impaired 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D production, secondary hyperparathyroidism, and acidosis. Bone biopsy may be needed to distinguish high- vs low-turnover disease. Denosumab in advanced CKD carries a boxed warning for severe hypocalcemia.
source: KDIGO CKD-MBD guidelines; Prolia FDA label (2024 BBW)

front: Between consecutive DXA patients, the technologist should:
back:
Use hand sanitizer before and after each patient

Hand hygiene (alcohol-based sanitizer or soap and water) between every patient is a CDC standard precaution. The technologist should also wipe down the scanner table and positioning aids with appropriate disinfectant between patients. These practices prevent transmission of infectious agents in the DXA suite. The 2022 ARRT BD specifications explicitly include infection control under Patient Care.
source: CDC standard precautions; ARRT 2022 Patient Care

front: The DXA scanner table and positioning aids should be cleaned:
back:
Between every patient with appropriate disinfectant

Equipment cleaning between every patient is standard infection control. Use the disinfectant approved by the scanner manufacturer to avoid damaging surfaces. Common surfaces include the table top, leg block, hip positioner, foam wedges, and any handheld controllers. The 2022 ARRT BD specifications explicitly include infection control as part of Patient Care, recognizing the technologist's role in pathogen prevention.
source: CDC infection control; ARRT 2022 Patient Care; manufacturer cleaning protocols

front: The universal femoral neck T-score reference used by all DXA manufacturers is:
back:
NHANES III (National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey III)

Per ISCD 2023, all DXA manufacturers use NHANES III White female young-adult reference data for femoral neck and total hip T-scores, regardless of patient sex or ethnicity. This standardization ensures comparable T-scores across manufacturers at the hip. Spine T-scores still use manufacturer-specific databases. The uniform White female reference at the hip simplifies WHO criteria application.
source: ISCD 2023 Adult Official Positions; NHANES III reference

front: Per ISCD 2023, when both hips are scanned, the diagnostic T-score is determined by:
back:
The lowest of four ROIs (left and right femoral neck and total hip)

Per ISCD 2023 (Tanner et al. bilateral hip position), the diagnostic T-score from bilateral hip scanning uses the LOWEST of the four ROIs (left femoral neck, left total hip, right femoral neck, right total hip). For monitoring, the MEAN total hip is used because it has the lowest precision error. This captures the worst-affected ROI for diagnosis while using the most reproducible measure for follow-up.

front: FRAX can be calculated without BMD when:
back:
BMD is unavailable; clinical risk factors alone are used

FRAX can be calculated using clinical risk factors alone when BMD is unavailable (less accurate but still useful for risk stratification). However, FRAX is most accurate with femoral neck BMD entered. FRAX is validated only for untreated adults aged 40-90; it should NOT be used in pediatric patients (under 40) or in patients on active treatment, where treatment effect distorts the predicted natural-history risk.
source: FRAX tool documentation; ISCD FRAX positions

front: BHOF treatment thresholds in osteopenic patients (US-specific FRAX) typically begin at:
back:
10-year major fracture probability of 20 percent OR hip probability of 3 percent

BHOF guidelines recommend pharmacologic treatment in osteopenic patients when 10-year major osteoporotic fracture probability is at least 20 percent OR 10-year hip fracture probability is at least 3 percent (US-specific FRAX). These thresholds are designed to be cost-effective and to balance treatment benefit against risk. Lower thresholds may be appropriate based on clinical judgment.
source: BHOF 2022 Clinician's Guide; FRAX treatment thresholds

front: The foot strap on a hip positioner is used to:
back:
Maintain the 15-25 degrees of internal rotation throughout the scan

The foot strap secures the foot in the hip positioner at 15-25 degrees of internal rotation, preventing inadvertent loss of rotation during the scan. Maintaining internal rotation throughout acquisition is critical for consistent femoral neck projection and reproducible follow-up scans. Without secure strapping, the foot may rotate externally during the scan, foreshortening the femoral neck and inflating BMD.
source: Manufacturer hip positioner specifications; ISCD hip acquisition

front: The total hip ROI in a proximal femur DXA scan includes:
back:
Femoral neck, trochanteric region, and intertrochanteric region

The total hip ROI encompasses the femoral neck, trochanteric (greater trochanter), and intertrochanteric (between trochanter and shaft) regions, providing an integrated measure. It does NOT include the femoral head (excluded due to acetabular overlap) or the femoral shaft beyond intertrochanteric. The total hip ROI is the preferred monitoring ROI in the hip due to its larger size and better precision than femoral neck alone.
source: ISCD 2023 hip reporting; manufacturer ROI definitions

topic: Femoral neck vs total hip precision
front: Which hip ROI has the LOWEST precision error and is therefore preferred for monitoring?
back:
Total hip (larger ROI, more reproducible)

Total hip has the lowest precision error (typically 1-2 percent CV) because it averages a larger anatomical area, making it less sensitive to small positioning variations. The femoral neck has slightly worse precision due to its smaller ROI and sensitivity to internal rotation. Ward's area has very poor precision and is not used for diagnosis or monitoring. ISCD recommends total hip for monitoring; femoral neck is used for diagnosis (lowest T-score) and FRAX input.

front: For optimal PA lumbar spine DXA, the spine should be:
back:
Straight and centered in the scan field

The lumbar spine must be straight and centered in the scan field for valid analysis. The patient lies supine with shoulders squared to the pelvis (no torso rotation), legs elevated on the block to flatten lordosis, and the spine aligned along the long axis of the table. Centering minimizes fan-beam magnification artifacts; straight alignment ensures the algorithm can drop horizontal intervertebral lines cleanly.

topic: Spine ROI top T12-L1 to L4-L5
front: The PA lumbar spine ROI should extend from approximately:
back:
T12-L1 disc space to L4-L5 disc space

The standard lumbar spine ROI extends from the T12-L1 disc space (top of L1) to the L4-L5 disc space (bottom of L4), encompassing L1-L4. This is the ISCD standard. T12 is excluded because it is thoracic; L5 is excluded due to frequent variant anatomy and overlap with sacral structures. The algorithm places horizontal intervertebral lines through the disc spaces between each labeled vertebra.

front: A vertebral compression fracture typically affects DXA BMD measurement how?
back:
Falsely elevates the measured BMD due to area reduction

A compression fracture reduces vertebral height while preserving most of the bone mineral content, concentrating the BMC into a smaller projected area. Since BMD equals BMC divided by Area, the smaller area inflates the measured BMD at the fractured vertebra. This is why fractured vertebrae must be excluded from analysis: their falsely elevated BMD would mask true low bone mass.
source: ISCD 2023 spine exclusion; standard DXA artifact references

front: Forearm DXA can be performed with the patient:
back:
Seated alongside the scanner, off the main table

Forearm DXA is typically performed with the patient seated beside the scanner with the forearm extended onto the scan surface. This positioning makes the forearm the ideal alternative when patients cannot lie supine (severe kyphosis, contractures) or exceed the table weight limit. The forearm is positioned pronated (palm down), straight, and centered, with the wrist supported.

front: The ultradistal radius differs from the 33% radius in that the ultradistal is:
back:
More trabecular (approximately 50-66 percent)

The ultradistal radius is approximately 50-66 percent trabecular bone, in contrast to the 33% radius which is approximately 95 percent cortical. The ultradistal site is acquired during forearm DXA but is NOT used for WHO-criteria diagnosis (the 33% radius is the only diagnostic forearm site per ISCD). The ultradistal site may be reported for research or comparison purposes.
source: ISCD 2023 forearm positions; standard bone composition

front: The mid-radius (50%) site in forearm DXA is located:
back:
At the midpoint of the radius

The mid-radius (also called the 50% radius) is at the midpoint of the radius, between the wrist and elbow. It is sometimes acquired but is not the diagnostic site (33% radius is). The 33% radius is at one-third of forearm length from the styloid end. Some clinical protocols report the mid-radius for cortical bone monitoring in addition to the 33% radius.

front: Bland-Altman plots in DXA precision studies are used to:
back:
Display the difference between paired measurements against their mean

Bland-Altman plots display the difference between paired measurements (Scan 2 minus Scan 1) on the y-axis against the mean of the two measurements on the x-axis. This visualizes any systematic bias and the spread of differences across the BMD range. Bland-Altman plots are standard tools for precision studies and method comparison.
source: Bland and Altman 1986; ISCD precision assessment references

front: Typical in vivo precision (RMS-CV%) at the total hip is approximately:
back:
1-2 percent

Typical in vivo precision at the total hip is 1-2 percent RMS-CV. Spine precision is similar (1-2 percent). Femoral neck precision is slightly worse (1.5-2.5 percent) due to smaller ROI and rotation sensitivity. Forearm precision is also in the 1-2 percent range. Each facility should measure its own precision to calculate the facility-specific LSC; manufacturer claims of less than 1 percent are typically phantom precision, not in vivo.

front: In DXA measurement, RANDOM error primarily affects:
back:
Precision (reproducibility)

Random error (variability from one measurement to the next under nominally identical conditions) primarily affects PRECISION. Systematic error (a consistent offset, like calibration drift) primarily affects ACCURACY. In DXA, random error sources include positioning variability and electronic noise. Systematic error sources include calibration drift, software algorithm changes, and reference database updates.

front: A Shewhart QC 'trend' is defined as:
back:
5 consecutive points moving in the same direction

A Shewhart 'trend' is 5 or more consecutive QC data points moving in the same direction (consistently increasing or decreasing), even if all values remain within the 1.5 percent control limit. A trend signals systematic drift requiring investigation. A 'shift' is 10 consecutive points on the same side of the mean (not necessarily monotonic). Both patterns indicate the scanner's calibration is moving away from baseline.
source: Lewiecki 2016 J Clin Densitom; Shewhart QC standards

front: Hologic spine phantoms are typically constructed of:
back:
Calcium hydroxyapatite (CaHA) embedded in epoxy resin

Hologic spine phantoms use four CaHA (calcium hydroxyapatite) vertebrae of varying densities embedded in an epoxy resin matrix that simulates soft tissue. This anthropomorphic design mimics the attenuation characteristics of real human bone in soft tissue, allowing realistic daily QC. GE Lunar systems use the Aluminum Spine Phantom (ASP). Both manufacturers' phantoms serve the same QC purpose using different physical designs.

front: Daily QC records should be retained for:
back:
Per regulatory requirements (typically 5+ years)

QC records should be retained per facility policy and regulatory requirements, typically at least 5 years and often indefinitely. QC logs document scanner performance over time, support medical-legal review of patient scans, and provide evidence of regulatory compliance (state radiation control programs, CMS, accreditation bodies). Modern DXA software stores QC electronically; backup procedures should preserve QC data alongside patient data.
source: ISCD QC standards; CMS and state regulatory requirements

front: Hologic DXA scanners use which dual-energy generation method?
back:
Rapid voltage switching between 100 and 140 kVp

Hologic DXA systems use rapid voltage switching between 100 kVp (low energy) and 140 kVp (high energy) within milliseconds during scanning, producing two distinct energy spectra for dual-energy subtraction. GE Lunar uses k-edge filtration with a cerium filter applied to a constant 76 kVp beam to achieve the dual energies. Both methods produce valid BMD measurements; absolute values differ between manufacturers and require cross-calibration when switching.

front: GE Lunar DXA scanners generate dual energies using:
back:
K-edge filtration with a cerium filter at constant 76 kVp

GE Lunar systems use a cerium k-edge filter applied to a constant 76 kVp X-ray beam. The cerium absorbs photons near its k-shell binding energy (about 40 keV), producing a beam with two distinct energy peaks (one below and one above the k-edge). Hologic uses rapid kVp switching instead. Both technologies enable the dual-energy attenuation comparison needed for BMD calculation.

front: Modern DXA X-ray tube heat capacity is rated in:
back:
Heat Units (HU) or kilojoules

X-ray tube heat capacity is rated in Heat Units (HU) or kilojoules. Heat dissipation is rarely a limiting factor for routine DXA because individual scans are short and use modest tube loading. Heavy clinical days with multiple body composition or pediatric scans may approach tube thermal limits in older equipment; modern tubes have sufficient capacity for typical workloads. Tube cooling is monitored automatically by the scanner.
source: Manufacturer tube specifications; standard X-ray physics

front: Typical DXA scan time for a lumbar spine acquisition on a modern fan-beam scanner is approximately:
back:
30 seconds to 2 minutes

Modern fan-beam DXA spine scans typically take 30 seconds to 2 minutes depending on scan mode and patient size. Standard mode is fastest; Thick mode is slower to maintain photon flux through larger patients. Pencil-beam scans take significantly longer (5-10 minutes per site). Total appointment time including positioning is typically 15-30 minutes for a full DXA visit (spine, hip, optional VFA and forearm).

front: DXA X-ray tube current is typically:
back:
Less than 1 mA at very low photon flux

DXA tube current is typically less than 1 mA (often 0.5-3 mA), much lower than general radiography (50-500 mA) or CT (100-500 mA). The low tube current contributes to DXA's very low patient dose (1-10 uSv per scan site). Scan time is longer than radiography to compensate for the low photon flux, but total exposure remains far below other X-ray modalities.
source: Manufacturer tube specifications; standard DXA physics

front: WHO 1994 selected the -2.5 SD T-score threshold for osteoporosis based on:
back:
Matching osteoporosis prevalence to lifetime hip fracture risk

The -2.5 SD threshold was chosen so that the prevalence of osteoporosis (women below the threshold) approximately matched the lifetime hip fracture risk in postmenopausal women (about 15-20 percent).
